title: Deep Contrastive Hashing with Proxy Guidance for Multi-Label Micro-Video Retrieval
authors: Yun Li, Rui Chen, Haoyi Sun, Ningyuan Zhao, Peiguang Jing, Xingyu Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: The explosive growth of micro-videos has intensified the demand for efficient hash-based retrieval techniques, where the inherent co-occurrence of semantic concepts necessitates effective modeling of multi-label correlations. However, label confusion caused by high-density information, along with the complex entanglement of multi-label dependencies, still poses significant challenges to generating robust hash codes. To address these issues, we propose a proxy-guided contrastive hashing network (DCPH), which integrates contrastive learning with proxy guidance to generate semantic-constrained hash representations for micro-videos. Specifically, to handle the frequent yet tricky visual differences, we design a heterogeneity-aware visual-to-semantic projection (HAVSP) component. HAVSP aggregates overall visual representations in the shared space via cross-modal cyclic projection, and introduces dual-constrained contrastive alignment to alleviate semantic ambiguity between visual features and textual prompts. To explicitly model multi-label dependencies, we develop a proxy-guided decompositional hashing (PGDH) component. PGDH dynamically decomposes label semantics into sequential binary codes via serial hash mappings, and incorporates multi-label proxy-center learning to optimize the commonality and mutual exclusivity among entangled micro-videos. Comprehensive experiments on a large-scale benchmark dataset demonstrate that DCPH achieves state-of-the-art retrieval performance, verifying its efficiency and accuracy in diverse micro-video retrieval.

title: A Survey of Functional Testing and Validation of Quantum Circuits
authors: Emma Andrews, Aruna Jayasena, Prabhat Mishra
publish: IEEE Design & Test
abstract: Quantum computing has the potential to solve a wide variety of classically intractable problems. However, the inherent complexity, probabilistic behavior, and fragility of quantum systems pose significant challenges to ensuring the correctness and reliability of quantum programs. In this survey, we present a comprehensive overview of functional testing and validation techniques aimed at addressing these challenges for quantum circuits. We begin by outlining the unique characteristics of quantum systems that complicate traditional testing methodologies, including the absence of classical oracles, non-determinism due to quantum measurement, and the exponential growth of the state space. We then categorize and analyze a wide range of functional testing and validation techniques that have emerged to meet these challenges, including mutation testing, fuzz testing, and coverage testing. We further explore advanced semantic and white-box methods such as property-based testing, metamorphic testing, equivalence checking, fault testing, assertions, and concolic testing. Specifically, we discuss the applicability of these testing techniques to quantum circuits and examine their limitations in the current NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) era.

title: BSTAR Parameter Correction and Improved Orbit Prediction Accuracy for Low-Earth Orbit Space Objects Based on LR-MS-TCN
authors: Bingxu Yao, Xin Chen, Bowen Yang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The rapid growth of low-Earth-orbit (LEO) satellites has intensified orbital congestion and collision risks, wherein accurate orbit prediction is critical for space situational awareness and mission safety. Atmospheric drag, represented by the BSTAR parameter in two-line element (TLE) sets, dominates the orbital uncertainty of LEO targets below 800 km. However, traditional statistical methods and shallow neural networks struggle with long-sequence nonlinear time-varying perturbations and error accumulation in long-horizon extrapolation. To address these limitations, this study proposes a local residual multi-scale temporal convolutional network (LR-MS-TCN) model for BSTAR parameter correction to enhance LEO orbit prediction accuracy. The model integrates a multi-scale feature fusion module to extract perturbation features at different temporal scales, adopts a dilated causal TCN backbone to capture long-range temporal dependencies, and introduces a local residual compensation branch with a gated fusion mechanism to strengthen the representation of short-term sensitive variations near the current epoch. Experiments are conducted on 16 years of TLE data of the FENGYUN-3A satellite, with MAE and RMSE as evaluation metrics. Results show that the proposed model achieves 1.6%, 17.1%, 23.7%, and 27.6% 3D RMSE improvement at 24 h, 48 h, 72 h, and 96 h prediction horizons, respectively, outperforming MSF-TCN, PlainTCN, SingleScaleTCN, LSTM, and GRU. It also maintains stable performance under quiet, storm, and strong geomagnetic storm conditions and exhibits favorable cross-target generalization on the FENGYUN-3B satellite. The LR-MS-TCN model effectively improves BSTAR correction accuracy and long-horizon orbit prediction stability, providing a reliable data-driven approach for high-precision LEO orbital propagation.

title: A Communication-Centric 6G–LLM Architecture for Scalable Tactical Autonomous Defense Vehicle Networks
authors: Kiran Khurshid, Shumaila Javaid, Nasir Saeed
publish: IEEE Network
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and emerging 6G networks introduces new opportunities for scalable coordination in tactical autonomous vehicle systems. This paper proposes a communication-centric hierarchical architecture for Tactical Autonomous Defense Vehicle Networks (TADVNs) that models the integration of edge-assisted Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning with 6G-enabled connectivity and semantic communication. The framework is designed to improve coordination efficiency, reduce communication overhead, and enhance latency resilience under increasing fleet-scale operation. Unlike conventional task-specific AI pipelines that rely on structured feature processing and rule-based coordination, the proposed approach incorporates semantic abstraction and context-aware decision support within a layered edge–cloud communication architecture. We evaluate communication and coordination performance via Monte Carlo simulations across fleet sizes of 5–30 vehicles under contested network conditions. Results indicate that at 30-vehicle scale, the 6G–LLM configuration achieves 75.2% latency reduction (29.1 ms versus 117.5 ms), a 68.7 percentage point increase in mission success rate (82.9% versus 14.2%), and an 88.6% reduction in communication overhead compared to a 5G-based conventional AI baseline. These findings demonstrate measurable benefits in coordination and communication when semantic reasoning is combined with low-latency 6G connectivity.

title: Fine-Grained Patching via Contention Balancer: A Practical Mitigation Strategy for Spectre Vulnerabilities Using SpecScope Contention Maps
authors: Najmeh Nazari, Banafsheh Saber Latibari, Behnam Omidi, Hosein Mohammadi Makrani, Fatemeh Movafagh, Seyede Elahe Hosseini Imeni, Chongzhou Fang, Abhijit Mahalanobis, Khaled N. Khasawneh, Houman Homayoun, Hossein Sayadi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Spectre attacks exploit speculative execution to leak sensitive data, especially in Artificial Intelligence (AI) workloads, through microarchitectural side channels, posing persistent challenges to modern processors. While advanced defenses like adaptive contention monitoring and resource isolation offer strong protection, they often require costly hardware redesigns. To provide a practical alternative, we introduce Contention Balancer, a fine-grained, software-level mitigation that leverages SpecScope’s automated gadget identification and contention mapping. By injecting carefully selected balancing instructions into speculative paths, Contention Balancer equalizes execution characteristics between transient and non-transient paths, effectively masking contentionbased side-channel signals without altering program behavior. This approach delivers immediate, low-overhead protection against Spectre vulnerabilities, offering a deployable and cost-effective solution for securing computing systems in the AI era.

title: Parasitics-Aware Framework for Integrated OTA Sizing and Layout Synthesis in FinFET Technologies
authors: Endalk Y. Gebru, Subhadip Ghosh, Ramesh Harjani, Sachin S. Sapatnekar
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Traditional operational transconductance amplifier (OTA) optimizers decouple sizing from layout synthesis, leading to poor results under layout-induced parasitics—a problem exacerbated in FinFET nodes. This work proposes a hierarchical methodology for FinFET nodes that maintains self-consistency between schematic-level and postlayout design, leveraging a SPICE-calibrated $g_{m} / I_{D}$ methodology that incorporates layout parasitics. Unlike machine-learning-based approaches that rely on heavy SPICE simulations and large datasets, our approach is computationally efficient and invokes SPICE sparingly. Experimental results across four OTA topologies demonstrate that performance of the sized circuit is greatly degraded after layout (up to 44% in UGF, 17 dB in DC gain, and 22 dB in CMRR), yet the systematic, closed-loop use of correction factors with selfconsistent $g_{m} / I_{D}$ methodology enables recovery of postlayout performance and yields designs that meet specifications. The entire flow requires only $\mathbf{1 0 - 1 5}$ full SPICE simulations, enabling thorough exploration of design space for high-quality solutions.

title: Reusing Assertion Properties as Hardware Checkers: Implementation and their Software and Hardware Recovery
authors: Czea Sie Chuah, Christian Appold, Tim Leinmueller
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Assertion Checkers are widely used in pre-silicon verification to catch bugs early, e.g. in simulation and formal verification environment, saving time and costs. These assertion checkers in the form of properties can be also reused as hardware checkers to validate the correct behaviour of real hardware infield. They enable detection of errors during runtime for errors that escaped the used verification approaches, e.g. for difficult to trigger corner cases in the hardware appearing under real world conditions. Additionally, errors introduced in manufacturing or due to hardware faults, e.g. caused through radiation or aging, can be detected with them. An advantage of using hardware checkers in post-silicon is they can be reused from pre-silicon assertion checkers, saving time and effort of creating them from scratch. Our work showcases the implementation of such checkers starting from their selection from pre-silicon properties and their optimization for use as checkers in hardware. Afterwards, we convert them into synthesizable hardware description language code and finally suggest a new interface to integrate the checkers into a processor design. We demonstrate our work on a 32-bit RISC-V processor NS31A on a FPGA development board, the Arty-7 100T CSG324 Xilinx and show checker inclusion in hardware needs only very little additional area. On top of that, we also shows suitable software and hardware recovery methods when a checker is raised. This enables reliable hardware operation even in case of errors and prevents undesired behavior happening.

title: HDL-DFGen: A Digital Filter Hardware Description Generation Framework
authors: Pedro T. L. Pereira, Eduardo Da Costa, Sergio Bampi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper presents HDL-DFGen, a framework for automatically generating digital filter architectures in VHDL and Verilog. The tool supports fixed FIR filters, adaptive filters from the LMS family, and novel reconfigurable architectures capable of implementing multiple algorithms within a single circuit. By combining parameterizable templates and reusable libraries, HDL-DFGen allows designers to specify key requirements, such as filter length, precision of integer and fractional parts, filter type, and architectural configuration (parallel, semi-parallel, or sequential), and obtain complete hardware descriptions in seconds. The proposed framework significantly reduces the time and effort required to design complex VLSI architectures, thus favoring the development of dedicated circuits for signal processing applications. HDL-DFGen enables the creation of high-order filters with scalable performance while preserving area and power efficiency. Results demonstrate that the automatically generated architectures achieve competitive metrics regarding circuit complexity, power consumption, and processing throughput.

title: A Robust Framework based on Embedding Projection Learning for Intelligent Process Monitoring_supp1-3697096.docx
authors: He Yan
publish: N/A
abstract: Effective process monitoring is essential for ensuring the safety and reliability of industrial systems. Although Neighborhood Preserving Embedding (NPE)-based methods have shown strong capability in modeling local data structures, they often neglect the influence of abnormal samples, leading to unreliable neighborhood weight estimation and bias projection subspace. To alleviate this issue, a robust framework termed Robust Adaptive Embedding Projection (RAEP) is proposed. Compared with existing NPE-based methods, RAEP introduces a flexible data representation to construct more reliable neighborhood relationships and employs a capped L2,1-norm with a “min” constraint to enhance robustness against outliers while adaptively distinguishing abnormal samples during learning. Furthermore, to solve the proposed formulation, an equivalent reformulation with theoretical justification is developed, along with an efficient iterative optimization algorithm. Experimental results on both numerical and real-world datasets demonstrate that RAEP outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of monitoring performance and robustness.

title: Wasserstein bounds for non-linear Gaussian filters
authors: Toni Karvonen, Simo Särkkä
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: Most Kalman filters for non-linear systems, such as the unscented Kalman filter, are based on Gaussian approximations. We use Poincaré inequalities to bound the Wasserstein distance between the true joint distribution of the prediction and measurement and its Gaussian approximation. The bounds can be used to assess the performance of non-linear Gaussian filters and determine those filtering approximations that are most likely to induce error.

title: The Conversational Exam: A Scalable Assessment Design for the AI Era
authors: Lorena A. Barba, Laura Stegner
publish: Computer
abstract: Traditional assessment methods collapse when students use generative artificial intelligence to complete work without genuine engagement. This article presents the conversational exam—a scalable oral examination format that restores assessment validity.

title: Megatrends in Biomedical AI for Health Care From Data Foundations to Clinical Deployment
authors: J. Ben Tamo, Micky C. Nnamdi, Benoit L. Marteau, Shaunna Wang, Wenqi Shi, Yasemin Akay, Metin Akay, May D. Wang
publish: Computer
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) in health care is shifting from incremental experimentation to systemic integration, reshaping clinical workflows, care delivery, and operational strategy. This article identifies eight converging technology trends across a unified data-to-decision pipeline defining the future of biomedical AI, driven by two megatrends: AI and automation.

title: Reliability Test Techniques in Tabu Search Detection for Enhancing BER Performance of Array Reader Bit-Patterned Magnetic Recording Systems
authors: Mutita Mattayakan, Natthakan Rueangnetr, Kittipon Kankhunthod, Chanon Warisarn
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Bit-patterned magnetic recording (BPMR) at ultra-high densities is strongly affected by inter-track interference (ITI). To cope with severe ITI, we introduce a reliability-testing mechanism with adaptive symmetric thresholding based on the distribution of log-likelihood ratios (LLRs) to effectively identify unreliable bits while controlling the computational complexity of the Tabu search (TS) detector. Additionally, the selected bits identified from the TS detection are employed to refine the original LLR values through a proposed soft-information adjustment (SIA) process. Moreover, we also present an LLR weighting scheme to further enhance the refined LLRs produced by the SIA process, thereby improving the performance of low-density parity-check decoding. Results indicate that our proposed technique can reduce the complexity of the TS detector by using a reliability-testing mechanism. The SIA can be effectively combined with an LLR weighting scheme, thereby improving bit-error rate performance over conventional BPMR systems.

title: AdvMPC: A Controllable and Efficient Adversarial MPC Framework for Safety Validation in Autonomous Driving
authors: Huihua Gao, Hong Chen, Ting Qu, Fang Xu, Ping Wang, Yunfeng Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Generating controllable, efficient, and physically feasible adversarial driving behaviors under dynamic interactions remains a fundamental challenge in the safety validation of autonomous driving systems. Leveraging the advantages of model predictive control (MPC) in receding-horizon optimization and constraint handling, this article proposes adversarial model predictive control (AdvMPC), a controllable and efficient adversarial MPC framework for safety validation in autonomous driving. The proposed framework enables background vehicles to generate continuous, executable, and highly adversarial behaviors under realism constraints. It consists of two core components: an adversarial risk modeling and evaluation module, which quantitatively characterizes interaction risk and enforces scenario rationality; and an optimization formulation and solution module, which enhances controllability, interpretability, and computational efficiency through a stage-guided problem construction strategy and an event-triggered warm-start mechanism. Simulation results demonstrate that AdvMPC can reliably generate challenging safety-critical scenarios across diverse dynamic interactions, while ensuring vehicle dynamics feasibility. Furthermore, the framework has been successfully deployed on a hardware-in-the-loop platform for autonomous driving testing.

title: From Space to Economy: Deep Learning Fusion of Remote Sensing Data for Sub-National GDP Estimation
authors: Thomas Stark, Michael Wurm, Simon Krenn, Wolfgang Sulzer, Eleanor Stokes, Karen C. Seto, Hannes Taubenböck
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Reliable and up-to-date sub-national data on the gross domestic product (GDP) data remain scarce in many regions across the globe. This limits the ability to analyse spatial economic disparities and to design evidence-based development policies. In this study, we investigate whether high-resolution GDP estimates can be derived from multi-source remote sensing and GIS inputs using a deep learning fusion framework. We combine optical day- and night-time satellite imagery, and auxiliary geospatial layers with several backbone architectures and fusion strategies to assess the robustness of multi modal learning for economic prediction. Across extensive experiments in Brazil, we evaluate multiple encoders (ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B3, SwinV2-T), alternative fusion layers (concatenation, attention pooling, graph-based MLP, mixture-of-experts), and diverse input modality combinations. Our best-performing model, an EfficientNet-B3 encoder with concatenation fusion using all available input modalities, achieves an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> value of 0.87 for GDP prediction at a spatial resolution of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {\text{5}\,\text{km} \times \text{5}\,\text{km}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, demonstrating that our multi modal approach effectively captures the complex relationships between spatial patterns and economic activity. These findings highlight the potential of multimodal remote sensing to complement traditional statistical sources by providing spatially consistent, high-resolution representations of economic activity.

title: Vulnerability Analysis of Multi-Timescale DC Microgrids under False Data Injection Attacks
authors: Qi Liu, Chengcheng Zhao, Wenxuan Ma, Jiming Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: This paper presents a vulnerability analysis for the multi-timescale DC microgrid (DCmG) against false data injection (FDI) attacks. Using singular perturbation theory, the system dynamics are decomposed into a fast boundary layer subsystem and a slow reduced-order model. Sufficient stability conditions for both subsystems are derived, and input-to-state stability (ISS) of the slow subsystem is established under attacks. Furthermore, a zonotopic reachability analysis is performed to estimate the worst-case state deviation and compute an analytical admissible attack indicator for safety assessment. Theoretical predictions are validated through both numerical simulations and full-hardware experiments. It is demonstrated that the multi-timescale property leads to new attack surfaces, and the analytical attack bounds are consistent with observed voltage deviations, which can effectively guide safe and secure control under cyber threats.

title: Near-Field Radio Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
authors: Wankyu Choe, Hyunwoo Park, Jeongwan Kang, Sunwoo Kim
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a near-field radio simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) method that explicitly leverages spherical-wave propagation for high-precision localization and sensing in sixth-generation networks. The proposed method redesigns the birth process of a multiple-model probability hypothesis density filter for landmark-type assignment by exploiting additional distance information available from spherical wavefronts. In addition, posterior Cram´er-Rao bounds are derived to quantify the resulting information gain for vehicle state estimation and mapping. Simulation results demonstrate that the method improves vehicle state estimation, mapping accuracy, and computational efficiency.

title: Introducing IEEE Collabratec
authors: N/A

title: Adaptive Graph-Guided Feature Decomposition for Unsupervised Multiview Feature Selection
authors: Aihong Yuan, Hong Lv, Jin Hu, Mengbo You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The exponential growth and heterogeneity of multiview data have made dimensionality reduction indispensable. Unsupervised multiview feature selection (UMFS) has emerged as a pivotal solution. However, existing embedded UMFS methods still face several challenges, including poor pseudo-label quality, inadequate modeling of nonlinear structures, and heavy reliance on hyperparameters. To address these challenges, we propose a novel UMFS method named adaptive graph-guided feature decomposition (AGFD) for UMFS. It integrates feature selection into a nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF) framework and introduces a view-adaptive weighting mechanism. Different from the method of directly learning the view similarity matrix, AGFD constructs a global similarity matrix by refining predefined similarity matrices using Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence, allowing it to better capture underlying nonlinear relationships across views. The adaptive design of AGFD reduces hyperparameter sensitivity and enhances interpretability. Extensive experiments on eight public multiview datasets demonstrate that AGFD outperforms existing methods, validating its effectiveness in UMFS tasks. The source code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ahyuan/AGFD</uri>

title: Mosaic Entropy: A Novel Widely-Applicable Measure for Two-Dimensional Irregularity Analysis_supp1-3697682.pdf
authors: Ying-ping Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Entropy-based descriptors are widely used for two-dimensional texture irregularity analysis. In counting-based formulations such as two-dimensional Dispersion Entropy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{DispEn}\_{2\mathrm{D}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), potential pattern counts grow rapidly with embedding dimension while available templates remain limited by image size, which can bias probability estimation and compress responses. Mosaic Entropy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{MosEn}\_{2\mathrm{D}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) is proposed, combining binary mosaic representation with conditional probability counting to stabilize statistics under limited template support. Experiments on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{WGN}\_{2\mathrm{D}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{MIX}\_{2\mathrm{D}}(p)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, Kylberg textures, and Kylberg ISO noise show that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{MosEn}\_{2\mathrm{D}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> improves image-size stability, reduces saturation as irregularity increases, and preserves distinguishable responses under realistic sensor noise, with lower computational overhead. It also maintains texture discrimination comparable to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{DispEn}\_{2\mathrm{D}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> without requiring an additional class parameter.

title: Design and characterization of a dense linear array EMAT based on spatial vertical winding coils for high-resolution imaging
authors: Zongjian Zhang, Yang Zheng, Zenghua Liu, Jidong Tan, Fei Xue, Peng Kang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: To address the limitations of electromagnetic acoustic transducers (EMATs) in high-resolution defect detection, this paper proposes a dense linear array EMAT based on spatial vertical winding coils (SVWCs) and an edge-constrained permanent-magnet array (EC-PMA). While ensuring high transduction efficiency for individual elements, this design constrains the element pitch to 1.5 mm, significantly increasing the number of elements within a finite aperture and enabling the development of a 32-element dense array EMAT. The array performance was systematically characterized in terms of frequency response, lift-off attenuation, channel consistency, and inter-element crosstalk. The results show that the elements exhibit broadband characteristics within the effective frequency range. Echo amplitudes decay exponentially with increasing lift-off, following a similar trend across different frequencies and materials. Channel consistency was evaluated using the relative pulse-echo sensitivity variation, and the results showed that 96.88% of the channels exhibited variations within ±3 dB, indicating good uniformity. In addition, inter-element crosstalk is closely related to both frequency and element spacing: it increases with frequency, reaches a maximum near 7 MHz, and decreases as the transmitter-receiver distance increases. By combining the developed array EMAT with a total focusing method incorporating angle weighting factors, clear imaging of multiple side-drilled holes with diameters of 2.0 mm, 1.0 mm, and 0.5 mm was achieved in an 80 mm-thick P91 steel block. These results verify the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed array EMAT for high-resolution defect imaging and provide a useful sensor foundation for advanced phased-array EMAT imaging.

title: DGRNET: A Spatiotemporal Attention Neural Network for Millimeter-Wave Radar HAR with Integrated Intensity Information
authors: Tianshi Dai, Bangbang Fu, Minhui Liu, Jian Guo, Yunpeng Zhang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) based on millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar point clouds has attracted increasing attention as a non-wearable solution due to its notable advantages in unobtrusive monitoring and privacy protection. However, conventional single-frame 3D radar point clouds often fail to provide sufficient discriminative information due to their inherent sparsity. To address this issue, this paper proposes DGRNET, a temporal dynamic graph convolutional network that incorporates signal-intensity dimensions and is specifically optimized for radar point clouds. The proposed network features two key designs. First, the feature convolution module is carefully structured to effectively fuse traditional 3D spatial point cloud features with additional intensity-based point features. Second, temporal information is explicitly introduced into the model, allowing the fusion of spatial and temporal features during extraction. Furthermore, intensity-based channel attention and temporal attention mechanisms are integrated to enhance the network’s representational capacity. Experimental results demonstrate that DGRNET achieves classification accuracies of 98.7%, 99.63%, and 99.54% on the m-Activity, 3DPCHM, and our own publicly available MVRHAR datasets, respectively, achieving the highest accuracy among comparable neural network models while maintaining a moderate model size (1.12M). In addition, we briefly introduce our self-collected mmWave radar point cloud HAR dataset and the corresponding preprocessing methodology, both of which play an essential role in evaluating the model’s performance.

title: Low Harmonic Alternating Convex-Concave Modulation Strategy for Multi-Port Matrix Converter
authors: Yunqing Hu, Fujin Deng, Wu Cao, Abdalbasit Mnider, Sayed Abulanwar, Frede Blaabjerg
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Multi-port converters (MPCs) have attracted increasing attention due to their advantages in integrating multiple energy sources, storages, and loads with high flexibility. However, excessive capacitors are a significant challenge for MPCs, which reduces the power density and reliability. In this article, a multi-port matrix converter (MPMC) and its alternating convex-concave modulation (ACCM) strategy is proposed. The proposed MPMC achieves direct power transmission between the high-frequency side and low-frequency side of ac port, which removes bulky electrolytic capacitors. The proposed ACCM strategy reconstructs the high-frequency voltage waveforms of the ac port into alternating convex-concave odd-function patterns, inherently suppressing the low-order harmonics of the three-phase currents. Simulation and experimental studies are conducted, and the results verify the effectiveness of the proposed MPMC and its ACCM strategy.

title: Unified Energy Function Tailored to Inverter-Based Resources with PI Controllers for Transient Stability Analysis
authors: Yifan Zhang, Hsiao-Dong Chiang, Yitong Li, Yang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The increasing penetration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) has fundamentally altered the transient stability characteristics of modern power systems. IBRs typically rely on proportional–integral (PI) controllers for synchronization and regulation, resulting in nonlinear swing equations that differ significantly from those of synchronous generators (SGs) and exhibit state-dependent damping. Consequently, although the classical energy function is often adopted in IBR analysis by analogy with SGs, it cannot be directly applied to IBRs with PI controller. A new energy function explicitly tailored to PI controller is proposed in this letter. It admits a unified form and can be applied to a class of nonlinear systems with PI controllers. Two representative cases are considered, including a grid-following (GFL) inverter and a DC-voltage-controlled grid forming (GFM) inverter, demonstrating less conservative and more effective estimation of the region of attraction (ROA). All findings are verified through hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) experiments.

title: Ethical Synthetic Text Generation for Vulnerable Populations Using Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey
authors: Tung Vu, Lam Nguyen, Quynh Dao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating synthetic text data, offering transformative potential for data augmentation, privacy preservation, and addressing data scarcity across sensitive domains. However, when the subjects of generated content involve populationsincluding children, patients, refugees, racial and ethnic minorities, LGBTQ+ individuals, and persons with disabilitiescritical ethical risks emerge, spanning privacy breaches, bias amplification, stereo-typical representation, and the production of harmful content. Despite growing interest in both synthetic data generation and AI safety, there remains no comprehensive survey that systematically examines the intersection of ethical synthetic text generation and vulnerable population protection. This paper addresses this gap by providing the first comprehensive survey of methods, frameworks, evaluation metrics, and open challenges in this domain. We review over 300 works spanning synthetic data generation, prompt engineering, LLM safety and alignment, fairness and bias mitigation, and privacypreserving techniques. We propose a taxonomy that classifies existing approaches along three axes: the generation pipeline stage (pregeneration, ingeneration, postgeneration), the ethical dimension addressed (privacy, fairness, safety, representation), and the target population. We critically analyze four systematic failure patterns in existing methodspopulation blindness, intersectionality gaps, evaluation circularity, and static ethical requirementsand provide a research gaps matrix mapping understudied populationdimension combinations. Furthermore, we introduce PromptGuard, a modular orchestrated prompting framework that integrates existing prompting techniquesincluding ChainofThought, TreeofThoughts, Constitutional AI, ReAct, and SelfCritiqueinto a principled sixmodule architecture designed for populationspecific ethical text generation. We demonstrate PromptGuard through three detailed case studies spanning LGBTQ+ youth mental health, clinical records, and refugee narratives. We additionally analyze the regulatory landscape (EU AI Act, COPPA, HIPAA), crosscultural ethical tensions, and model collapse risks. Finally, we distill practical guidelines for practitioners and outline future research directions toward more inclusive, culturally adaptive, and communitydriven ethical AI systems.

title: Machine Learning for Metamaterial Design: Methods, Challenges, and Future Perspectives
authors: Saeid Karamzadeh, Lida Kouhalvandi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Metamaterials and metasurfaces enable advanced control of electromagnetic waves for applications such as cloaking, beam steering, and sensing, but their design is limited by nonlinear responses, high-dimensional parameter spaces, and expensive full-wave simulations. Machine learning (ML) has recently emerged as an effective approach to overcome these limitations by enabling data-driven forward modeling, inverse design, surrogate optimization, and adaptive control of electromagnetic structures. This paper surveys ML-based design methodologies for metamaterials and metasurfaces, focusing on neural network models for forward prediction, inverse mapping, topology optimization, and programmable metasurface control. Particular attention is given to reconfigurable intelligent surfaces, where ML enables scalable configuration in dynamic wireless environments, as well as emerging biomedical sensing applications. Finally, we analyze key challenges including generalization, interpretability, and integration with electromagnetic solvers, and outline future directions such as physics-informed learning and real-time adaptive metasurface systems.

title: Comparative Insights into NeRF 3D Scalability
authors: Efstratios Kakaletsis, Grigorios Aris Cheimariotis, Panos K. Papadopoulos, Dimitrios Zarpalas
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: 3D reconstruction for large scale indoor and outdoor environments constituted a challenging problem in inverse graphics and computer vision research community. The scalability issue that concerns only the geographic dimensions of the reconstructed scene in terms of scale is a inevitable bottleneck. This could be confronted by state-of-the-art methods which a comparative study should be reveal new insights of the digital environment representation. In this paper, a comparative study is facilitated including few literature methods with accordance by common evaluation metrics and computational complexity. A novel loss function referring to the scene details is also discussed by accessing the density variable of the NeRF multilayer perceptron output as alternative research direction. To this end, it presents efficiency advantange in terms of robustness to noise, scale and sentivity to distortions combined with sufficiency on accessed GP-GPUs memory and footprint in several datasets while it was validated and compared with state-of-the-art Inf-NeRF, Grid-NeRF and Nerfacto techniques.

title: IEEE Robotics and Automation Society Information
authors: N/A

title: LOPAL: Local Performance-Aware Active Learning from Imperfect Demonstrations
authors: Johannes Heidersberger, Shail Jadav, Dongheui Lee
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Learning from Demonstration (LfD) enables intuitive robot skill acquisition by allowing robots to learn directly from human task demonstrations. However, current methods often fail to address the fact that due to suboptimal and inconsistent human behavior, the quality of the demonstration can vary within each demonstration. Therefore, we introduce LOPAL (LOcal Performance-aware Active Learning), an active learning approach that leverages this local demonstration quality information. Our approach consists of two synergistic components. First, a local performance-driven LfD method uses a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to encode both the demonstrated trajectories and their associated local quality assessments. This enables the generation of trajectories that outperform the imperfect demonstrations by utilizing complementary local data of high performance. Second, active data acquisition allows to improve beyond the imperfect demonstrations by collecting additional informative samples. In areas missing good data, the user is actively requested to provide corrections through a shared autonomy (SA) mechanism, while the robot autonomously executes the learned behavior. The efficacy of LOPAL was validated in both a simulation and a real-world experiment. The results from a real-world pipe inspection task showed that the proposed approach can achieve up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$27.31\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> improvement in task performance while also reducing the effort required to collect the demonstrations.

title: A Feynman-Sprinkler-Like Electromagnetic Wind Energy Harvester for High-Speed Wind Fields_supp2-3695821.mp4
authors: Biao WANG
publish: N/A
abstract: Distributed Internet of Things (IoT) nodes powered by wind energy harvesters (WEHs) have enabled long-term, maintenance-free environmental monitoring in remote and extreme scenarios. However, the large fluctuations in wind speed may cause structural fatigue and dynamical instability of conventional WEHs. To achieve stable and durable operation under high wind speeds (up to 25 m/s), this study reports a Feynman-sprinkler-like WEH (FSL-WEH). In contrast to conventional WEHs that rely on turbine blades directly driven by the wind, the FSL-WEH harnesses the reaction force generated by airflow expelled through Feynman nozzles to produce rotational motion. Simulation and experiments indicate that the FSL-WEH achieves a maximum power output of 4.34 W and a peak power density of 66.77 kW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at a wind speed of 25 m/s. Moreover, the FSL-WEH successfully powers an environmental monitoring system, which wirelessly transmits temperature and humidity data to connected terminals, validating the operational reliability and durability of the device in extremely high wind speeds and high-humidity conditions. This study provides a promising solution for scavenging wind energy to power distributed environmental sensor networks under high-wind, high-humidity conditions.

title: Networked Spatiotemporal Optimal Control and DC-PPO Reinforcement Learning for Combating Malware Spread in IoUT_supp1-3692250.pdf
authors: Xiaojing Zhong
publish: N/A
abstract: Facing the challenges of spatiotemporally coupled malware propagation and limited defense resources in the Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT), this paper proposes an integrated defense framework combining networked modeling, optimal control, and reinforcement learning. A networked spatiotemporal propagation model is established using the graph Laplacian, incorporating spatial heterogeneity to overcome the limitations of traditional ODE/PDE approaches. A regionalized optimal control framework is then derived, enabling dynamic, risk-based resource allocation. To address environmental uncertainties, a model-free Dual-Clip PPO (DC-PPO) algorithm is designed, which learns near-optimal control policies without a precise system model. Simulations show that our model captures realistic propagation dynamics, the control strategy significantly reduces infection rates and cost, and DC-PPO demonstrates superior generalization and robustness across various network topologies, achieving an error of less than 3% compared with that of model-based optimal control. This work provides a rigorous and practical intelligent control solution for IoUT malware defense.

title: Optimizing Hardware Acceleration for Learned Image Compression with Parallel Context Model_supp1-3697680.pdf
authors: Zhengwei Qi
publish: N/A
abstract: Learned image compression (LIC) methods have approached or even surpassed advanced traditional methods (e.g., AVIF, JPEG-XL) in terms of compression bitrate and quality. Among them, autoregressive context models (ARCMs) have greatly improved the accuracy of entropy models, but strict Z-ordered serial decoding in ARCM leads to unacceptable latencies (∼ 50× latency overhead). Parallel context models achieve rate-distortion performance improvement close to ARCM while requiring only ∼ 10% − 40% of the latency overhead, but also introduce additional computational redundancy. In addition, most research ignore the en-/de-coding speed and only focus on the optimization of rate-distortion (RD). To tackle the above challenges, this paper optimizes the LIC model in both channel dimension and spatial dimension. In the channel dimension, considering the channel-wise priority characteristics in LIC models, slimmable neural network and variable bitrate optimizations are introduced to determine the execution of sub-models based on the dynamically available resources, achieving joint optimization of rate-distortion-complexity. In the spatial dimension, the computational redundancy of parallel context model (e.g., the structured redundancy of context features) is optimized, and the corresponding computation mode is unified with the existing modes (e.g., transposed convolution) in LIC models. Parameterized accelerator is designed for evaluation. Experimental results show that slimmable optimization realizes multiple RD trade-offs within single LIC model. Hardware/Software optimization of LIC model with Checkerboard context model achieve ∼ 1.97 − 4× performance improvement. Slimmable channel optimization achieves 1.52 − 2.07× gains to eliminate tail latency in computation module.

title: Power-Handling Enhancement in a Ka-Band Relativistic Cherenkov Oscillator via Distributed Impedance Engineering and Axial Extraction Circuit Topology_supp1-3695881.pdf
authors: Dongyang Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: This article presents a microwave circuit-level approach to enhance the power-handling capability of an overmoded Ka-band relativistic Cherenkov oscillator (RCO) operating under low guiding magnetic fields. Distributed impedance engineering is implemented through a segmented energy extraction slow wave structure (SEE-SWS), enabling spatially distributed beam energy extraction and suppression of localized resonant surface fields. In addition, a novel axial extraction cavity (AEC), functioning as an integrated microwave extraction circuit, provides efficient beam energy extraction, intrinsic <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {TM_{01}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> output mode purification, and effective load sharing with the SWS. The design is optimized using a neural-network-based genetic algorithm (NNGA). Particle-in-cell simulations show that, compared with the prototype, the maximum surface emission field is reduced from 3.38 to 0.94 MV/cm, while the output power is increased by approximately 25%. Experiments demonstrate that the device delivers a 620-MW, 20-ns flat-top microwave pulse at 29.4 GHz under a 0.62-T guiding magnetic field, with no pulse shortening, outperforming the prototype (400 MW, 7 ns). Moreover, stable 50-Hz repetitive operation for 4 s is achieved without active water cooling. These results highlight the effectiveness of distributed impedance engineering and axial extraction circuit design for power-handling enhancement in high-power millimeter-wave sources, providing a general microwave circuit-level methodology for overmoded millimeter-wave oscillator design.

title: Joint Topology Control, Routing, and Link Mode Selection via HRL and NSGA-III+ in OA-UWSNs_supp1-3697257.pdf
authors: Wenfeng Li
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper addresses the challenges of topology control, multi-hop routing, and link mode selection in optical-acoustic hybrid underwater wireless sensor networks (OA-UWSNs) by proposing a joint optimization framework that integrates hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) with an improved non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm (NSGA-III+). The OA-UWSN is formulated as a multi-objective graph optimization problem subject to connectivity, mode allocation, and reliability constraints. At the topology and link layers, the HRL-based approach employs a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm at the upper level to assign connectivity weights to network links and incorporates a minimal connectivity restoration mechanism to construct feasible subgraphs. At the lower level, Q-learning is adopted to enable adaptive selection between acoustic and optical transmission modes for each link. For the routing layer, NSGA-III+ is developed to achieve multi-objective route optimization, leveraging segmented path encoding, a dynamically weighted fitness function, and a multidimensional crowding adjustment strategy to enhance both the convergence rate and the diversity of the solution set. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed HRL+NSGA-III+ framework consistently outperforms existing baseline methods in terms of convergence speed, energy efficiency, latency reduction, and link quality, and exhibits robust performance across various network scales.

title: Real-Time Edge Semantics for Drone Swarms via FPGA Perception and On-Device LLMs
authors: Zhaoqi Wang, Wade A. Fortney, Peter O. Forcha, Yu Feng, Gabriel Bendix, Christophe Bobda
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Real-time semantic understanding for drone swarms demands robust multi-object perception and high-level reasoning under tight size, weight, power, and bandwidth constraints. Existing approaches either offload computation to cloud or ground stations, achieving rich semantics at the cost of latency and fragile links, or rely on lightweight on-board trackers that produce only low-level bounding boxes and trajectories. We bridge this gap with an edge-native pixels-to-semantics pipeline that combines a streaming FPGA front-end with an on-device large language model (LLM). A near-sensor FPGA accelerator on a Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC performs real-time multi-object tracking and emits a compact structured state with stable IDs, kinematic summaries, and interaction cues; a fine-tuned, quantized LLM on the on-board ARM cores consumes this state to infer swarmlevel behaviors, inter-object relations, and potential risks without processing raw pixels or relying on network connectivity. On a KV260 platform, the pipeline achieves an end-to-end visual latency of 66.7 ms per frame at $320 \times 240$, operates at approximately 2.9 W, and uses less than 2% of the device’s LUT/FF resources and a single BRAM block. We compare our system against hybrid and full-offloading architectures in latency, bandwidth, and power, and against representative FPGA-based visual frontends in hardware footprint. Finally, we evaluate the on-board LLM against an online ChatGPT 5.1 Auto baseline on a real multi-UAV rounding sequence, showing that the self-deployed model achieves zero error in corrected-count and mission-type prediction while the general-purpose model exhibits substantial errors, yielding a practical low-SWaP solution for semantic scene understanding in drone swarms.

title: Fault Tolerant Design of IGZO-based Binary Search ADCs
authors: Paula Carolina Lozano Duarte, Sule Ozev, Mehdi Tahoori
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Thin-film technologies such as Indium Gallium Zinc Oxide (IGZO) enable Flexible Electronics (FE) for emerging applications in wearable sensing, personal health monitoring, and large-area systems. Analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) serve as critical sensor interfaces in these systems. Yet, their vulnerability to manufacturing defects remains poorly understood despite unipolar technologies’ inherently high defect densities and process variations compared to mature CMOS technologies. We present a hierarchical fault injection framework to characterize defect sensitivity in Binary Search ADCs implemented in n-type only technologies. Our methodology combines transistor-level defect characterization with system-level fault propagation analysis, enabling efficient exploration of both single and multiple fault scenarios across the conversion hierarchy. The framework identifies critical fault-sensitive circuit components and enables selective redundancy strategies targeting only the most sensitive components. The resulting defect-tolerant designs improve fault coverage from 60% to 92% under single-fault injections and from 34% to 77.6% under multi-fault injection, while incurring only 4.2% area overhead and 6% power increase. While validated on IGZO-TFTs, the methodology applies to all emerging unipolar technologies.

title: An Automated Compilation Flow for Efficient GNN Inference on Edge Heterogeneous Platform
authors: Haoyang Fan, Sameh Gobriel, Nilesh Jain, Naveena Padmaraju, Chaunte W Lacewell, Viktor K. Prasanna
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: To support the surging demands for local AI inference, modern edge platforms have evolved into heterogeneous System-on-Chips (SoCs) integrating diverse Processing Units (PUs), including CPU, GPU, and NPU. These architectures offer unique potential to accelerate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), which are critical for local applications such as code assistants, by matching diverse PUs to the model’s hybrid compute- and memory-intensive phases. However, existing frameworks typically execute on a single PU or rely on rigid, coarse-grained partitioning. Such limitations hinder the effective exploitation of hardware heterogeneity, leading to suboptimal resource utilization, particularly for complex GNN models. In this paper, we propose FUSE (Fine-grained Unified Scheduling Engine), an automated compilation flow that abstracts and decomposes GNNs into fine-grained functional primitives, enabling better exploration of hardware heterogeneity. Unlike prior works that treat data and hardware separately, FUSE explores a unified design space that jointly optimizes data partitioning and finegrained hardware mapping. To efficiently explore the unified design space, FUSE implements a systematic online search engine guided by an offline-profiled hardware cost model. This engine prunes the design space and performs hierarchical search to balance workloads across heterogeneous PUs, ultimately generating an efficient execution plan tailored to the input. We evaluate FUSE on Intel AI PC SoCs using 3 widely-used GNN models and 3 datasets. FUSE outperforms single-device baselines by up to $2.67 \times$ and achieves speedups of up to $1.67 \times$ over state-of-the-art coarse-grained schedulers, while maintaining higher PU utilization.

title: An Adaptive PI-Based Current Balancing Control Method for the Unbalanced Three-Phase LLC Resonant Converter
authors: Hyun-Jin Jin, Tae-Seok Kang, Yeong-Jun Choi
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive PI-based current balancing control method for three-phase LLC resonant converters. To analyze the unbalanced current caused by resonant-component parameter deviations, three-phase equivalent circuits of the Y-connected and Δ-connected transformer configuration are derived, and the effects on the magnitude and phase of the unbalanced currents are analyzed. The proposed control method dynamically adjusts the phase angle of the resonant tank input voltage under various conditions of parameter variations. Through this, the magnitude and phase error of each phase current are minimized, and the current balancing performance is improved. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed method, experiments are conducted under load change and input voltage change conditions. In addition, the performance improvement of the proposed method is confirmed through comparison with PI- and sliding mode control (SMC)-based control methods. The experimental results show that the unbalance factor is reduced and the output current ripple also decreases when the proposed balancing control is applied.

title: Multi-Stage Optimization of Multi-Truck-Drone Cooperative Emergency Delivery Under Progressive Road Recovery
authors: Hailong Li, Ya Li, Jiawen Zhang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study investigates emergency material delivery with truck-drone teams when road accessibility is restored progressively after a disruption. Existing disruption-aware routing models typically optimize against a fixed accessibility snapshot and therefore cannot explain how service responsibility should be reassigned as the network recovers. We propose a multi-stage recovery-aware coordination framework in which progressive road recovery acts as an endogenous driver of mode assignment. As reachability expands over time, this process reshapes the service boundary between trucks and drones. The model further incorporates release-time-anchored deprivation accounting and an explicit distinction between truck visitation and truck service with sortie-based synchronization, thereby aligning humanitarian-loss measurement and operational coordination with post-disaster dynamics. To solve the problem, an exact mixed-integer benchmark is used on small instances and a stage-wise adaptive large neighborhood search is developed for larger ones. On the main three-seed 40-node calibrated instances, dynamic cooperation reduces the mean seed-wise scalarized objective by 31.8% relative to the truck-only baseline (no drone service) and by 61.5% relative to the static-strong truck-drone baseline (stage-1 mode preferences frozen during recovery). The stage-share results showsubstantial early drone usage together with a steadily increasing truck share as accessibility recovers, and the n40 ablation indicates that removing the mode-mutation operator causes the largest mean degradation among the tested operator removals (+45.5%). Overall, the results indicate that the main value of truck-drone cooperation lies not in adding drones per se, but in preserving the flexibility to reassign service modes as recovery unfolds.

title:
Toward Suppression of Bias Instability in Monolayer WSe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Top-Gate pFETs
authors: Yu-Wei Hsu, Chen-Hsun Hsu, Ching-Shuan Huang, Nien-En Chiang, Yun-Cheng Liu, Ting-Hua Wei, Sin-Yue Lee, Chris Yeh, I-Chih Ni, Hung-Li Chiang, Chih-I Wu, Tsung-En Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: This work presents the first systematic investigation of the bias instability of monolayer (1L) WSe2 top-gate p-type field-effect transistors (pFETs). A multilayer gate-stack is proposed to address the unique reliability challenges of 2D semiconductors, leveraging their van der Waals (vdW) interface for low interface trap density (D ${}_{\text {it}}\text {)}$ and improved bias stability. The proposed stack integrates a 1 nm post-oxidized physical vapor deposition (PVD) AlOx interfacial layer (IL) to enhance hole density and suppress hydroxyl-related defects, a two-step atomic layer deposition (ALD) temperature for Hf/Zr-based higher- $\kappa $ dielectrics to balance dielectric strength and channel degradation, and post-metallization annealing (PMA) for passivation of vacancy and hydrogen-related traps. With an equivalent oxide thickness (EOT) scaled to 0.8 nm, the fabricated long-channel devices achieve near-ideal subthreshold swing (S.S.) (S.S. $\approx$ 65 mV/dec) and negligible hysteresis at room temperature. The extrapolated lifetime projection indicates excellent bias stability, with the threshold voltage shift ( $\Delta $ V ${}_{\text {T}}\text {)}$ remaining below 90 mV under negative bias stress (NBS) and below 70 mV under positive bias stress (PBS) after ten years of operation at an effective stress field $\vert $ E ${}_{\text {ox}}\vert =5$ MV/cm. These results establish a scalable and thermally compatible gate dielectric integration for low-power 2D CMOS logic applications.

title: DEMO Divertor Target Mock-Ups With K-Doped Tungsten Laminates Monoblocks
authors: Francesco Crea, R. De Luca, S. Roccella, D. Caprini, M. Cerocchi, P. Lorusso, L. Verdini, A. Galatanu, J. H. You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Tungsten (W) is the preferred plasma-facing material (PFM) for European demonstration fusion power plant (EU-DEMO) divertor target. At plasma operative conditions, such as high temperatures, high heat fluxes (HHFs), particle fluxes, and neutron irradiation, W is subject to recrystallization and to embrittlement. A proper doping of W with Potassium (K) can help improve the PFM properties. Nano K-bubbles dispersed at the grain boundaries of W can increase mechanical resistance by hindering grain growth, ultimately leading to the suppression of recrystallization. Newly developed K-doped W laminates (KdWLs) materials were produced by the National Institute of Materials Physics (NIMPs) by means of the field-assisted sintering technique (FAST). At the Special Technologies Laboratories of ENEA Frascati (ENEA-TES), water-cooled ITER-like divertor target mock-ups equipped with KdWL monoblocks were fabricated. The KdWL monoblocks were joined to CuCrZr International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER) grade (CuCrZr-IG) cooling pipes by hot radial pressing (HRP) with an oxygen-free copper (OFC, Cu) interlayer manufactured by casting. Nondestructive examination (NDE) by ultrasonic inspection has been performed to assess the quality of the fabrication process and to analyze the structural integrity of the KdWL mock-up after HHF tests. The recent research activity is evaluating whether KdWL can represent a potential candidate armor material for a future water-cooled EU-DEMO divertor target. A dedicated qualification activity of the KdWL divertor target design is currently ongoing, and a mock-up has been produced to withstand cyclic loading up to 2000 cycles at 20 MW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. In this work, new KdWL monoblocks were obtained following the optimization of the fabrication process. These monoblocks have been used to fabricate two new divertor target mock-ups to be tested, respectively, at thermal fatigue and overload.

title: 4D Imaging in ISAC Systems: A Framework Based on 5G NR Downlink Signals
authors: Haoyang Weng, Haisu Wu, Hong Ren, Cunhua Pan, Jiangzhou Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a key enabler for sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks, supporting spectrum sharing and hardware integration. Beyond communication enhancement, ISAC also enables high-accuracy environment reconstruction and imaging, which are crucial for applications such as autonomous driving and digital twins. This paper proposes a 4D imaging framework fully compliant with the 5G New Radio (NR) protocol, where each sensing BS jointly estimates the three spatial coordinates and the BS-referenced radial velocity of each observed scatterer, ensuring direct compatibility with practical cellular deployments. Specifically, we develop an end-to-end processing chain that covers waveform generation, echo processing, and multi-BS point cloud fusion. Furthermore, we introduce Zoom-OMP, a coarse-to-fine sparse recovery algorithm for high-resolution angle estimation that achieves high accuracy with reduced computational cost. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves robust 4D parameter estimation performance together with superior spatial reconstruction quality compared to conventional benchmarks.

title: KGDNet: Knowledge-Guided Decoupled Modeling of Heterogeneous Change Mechanisms for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Zongyu Yang, Haitao Li, Haiyan Gu, Yi Yang, Shaopeng Ding, Haozhu Kong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-resolution remote sensing change detection methods typically rely on single-modal image features, making them highly susceptible to pseudo-changes induced by factors like illumination, shadows, and seasonal variations. Moreover, most approaches adopt a unified binary change paradigm, overlooking the heterogeneous mechanisms of change, such as appearance and disappearance. To address these limitations, this paper presents KGDNet, a knowledge-guided tri-branch network designed for decoupled modeling of heterogeneous change mechanisms in change detection (CD). KGDNet leverages annotation-free monocular depth maps as geometric priors and, through the Guided Alignment and Fusion Module (GAFM), upgrades 2D features to 3D geometry-aware representations. This enhances the discrimination of genuine land-cover changes while effectively suppressing pseudo-changes at the source. The network explicitly decouples three core mechanisms—appearance, difference, and disappearance—using specialized parallel branches to capture distinct change patterns. Additionally, the difference branch dynamically corrects and refines prior knowledge, mitigating contamination from low-quality depth cues. To efficiently process high-resolution imagery, we further propose a Multi-Head Latent Spatiotemporal Attention (MH-LSTA) module that compresses and remaps features in a latent space, substantially reducing computational and memory costs while enhancing the modeling of global spatiotemporal relations. This yields a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Comprehensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that KGDNet outperforms representative state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in key metrics, delivering superior detection accuracy and robustness. This study proposes a knowledge-guided decoupled modeling paradigm that integrates prior knowledge with heterogeneous change mechanisms to enhance the interpretability of change representations. The code and data will be released upon publication at https://github.com/yangzy1217/KGDNet.

title: Enhancing Speed of Piezoelectric Inchworm Actuator by a Monolithic Duple-Step Driving-Clamping Mechanism
authors: Hongchen Gao, Jie Zhu, Tao Chen, Lining Sun, Jizhu Liu, Mingxiang Ling
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Piezoelectric inchworm stepping motors have attracted much attention due to their motion stability and large thrust. However, simplifying structural complexity while balancing compromised performance is challenging. We propose a piezoelectric inchworm linear actuator with a duple-step driving principle in a single stepping cycle. This is realized by designing a monolithically integrated driving-clamping flexure stator, thus the motion speed is significantly enhanced with relatively large output thrust and self-locking capability at rest. A kinetostatic modeling is conducted to optimally match the compliance between the driving and clamping units. A high motion speed of 22.5 mm/s@220 Hz is experimentally measured with the motion resolution of 53 nm. The prototype also exhibits stable forward and backward motions with a thrust of 14.5 N and a vertical load capability exceeding 10 kg. The enhanced motion speed with relatively large output thrust observed is attributed to the duple-step motion mode by efficiently recycling elastic deformation of the monolithic driving-clamping stator. The compliant stator also enables an amplified stepping size of 2 <inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> 63 <inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m with relatively high dynamic bandwidth (resonance frequencies of 906.1 Hz for the driving unit and 3606 Hz for the clamping units).

title: Efficient and Effective Personalized In-Context Learning for On-device Large Model Services
authors: Huili Wang, Yuanhong Huang, Zhiyang Hu, Qing Li, Pingyi Fan, Yongfeng Huang, Shangguang Wang, Tao Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Recently, on-device large models (e.g., 7B parameter LLMs and LVLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world services such as mobile assistants, edge computing, and IoT systems, where low latency and resource efficiency are critical. Since large models trained on general-domain data are typically suboptimal for downstream services, In-Context Learning (ICL) technique is widely used to enhance their task-specific service performance without extra tuning. However, effective ICL methods generally necessitate a sufficient amount of supervised data to provide abundant service-related information, which usually leads to a long context (i.e., more texts or images input) for models to process. This long context issue can significantly degrade the real-time inference efficiency and also impact the information utilization effectiveness, particularly for on-device large models with limited long context modeling capacities. To tackle this challenge, we propose a Personalized Knowledge Refinement (PKR) framework to achieve efficient and effective ICL for on-device large models. Specifically, we first introduce a personalized knowledge extraction module, which analyzes the inference behaviors of the target model on small-scale supervised data and then convert these behavioral patterns into personalized service-specific knowledge as the instruction context. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive knowledge filtration mechanism to model the informativeness of the extracted knowledge and eliminate the redundant ones, further improving the knowledge encoding efficiency per unit of context length. Experiments based on 14 benchmarks spanning both textual and visual task services, and 4 large models, demonstrated that PKR consistently improves task accuracy while reducing context length and inference latency, making it a practical solution for real-world on-device services. Codes are released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wanghl21/PKR</uri>.

title: Performance Evaluation and Optimization of TiO₂-Based Nano-Modified Natural Ester–Mineral Oil Blends for Transformer Insulation using PCA-Integrated MCDM Techniques
authors: S Sharmila Devi, R. V. Maheswari, P. Senthil Kumar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Mineral oil has been widely used in power transformers; however, its environmental limitations have led to increasing interest in biodegradable alternatives such as natural esters. In this study, mineral oil is blended with rice bran oil and groundnut oil, and TiO₂ nanoparticles (0.02–0.1 g/L) are introduced to enhance insulation performance. The experimental results show that the nano-modified blends improve breakdown voltage from 38–47 kV to 41–49 kV, with the best performance observed at 0.05 g/L. A reduction in viscosity is also obtained, which supports better cooling. At the same time, higher flash and fire points indicate improved thermal safety. Ageing analysis at 120 °C for 30 days shows that the nano-modified oils retain better dielectric and thermal properties than the base oils. AFM results further confirm reduced surface degradation and improved fiber structure of kraft paper. In addition, a PCA-based integration of GRA, TOPSIS, and VIKOR provides a consistent ranking of alternatives and identifies S9 as the optimal blend. Overall, the results indicate that the proposed nano-modified ester–mineral oil blends are a viable alternative for transformer insulation.

title: Two-Dimensional Heat Transfer-Based Modeling and Experimental Validation for LCSR-Plunge Test Conversion
authors: Yanyong Song, Geyi Su, Xiang Wang, Yongkang Xia, Mingxu Su
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This study aims to establish a two-dimensional numerical model of a resistance temperature detector (RTD) based on heat transfer theory, and to evaluate the impact of internal and external factors (filler materials, sensor sizes, and flow velocities) on the sensor dynamic characteristics through computational simulation. Furthermore, a corrected conversion relationship between the loop current step response (LCSR) method and plunge tests has been established and validated. The proposed two-dimensional model exhibits consistent applicability under diverse conditions of filling material, sensor size, and flow velocity, with simulation relative errors under 3.44%—a level of accuracy markedly superior to that achievable with conventional one-dimensional models. Accordingly, an in situ LCSR-based device was developed, with which, the LCSR-Plunge test conversion relationship was further experimentally validated in a custom-built rotating water tank using industrial-grade RTDs (2.0 and 3.0 mm in radius) at flow velocities of 0.3, 0.6, and 1.0 m/s. The maximum deviation between the corrected converted response time and the direct plunge test results was observed to be 8.53%, demonstrating compliance with relevant nuclear instrumentation standards.

title: Enabling Low-Power Decoding of Ambient Signals at Backscatter Tags for Personal IoT
authors: Longzhi Yuan, Hangcheng Cao, Wei Gong, Yuguang Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: We present the design and implementation of PassiveFSK, a system that can decode multiple ambient signals in frequency shift keying (FSK) method with low power consumption. The key enabler is the biased tuning of the RF filter, which narrows the passband and creates a one-to-one mapping between the frequency offset and the envelope amplitude. Consequently, the tag can directly obtain the instantaneous frequency offset of RF signals for decoding using a passive envelope detection circuit, rather than relying on the traditional down-conversion approach. We showcase how the tag processes and decodes BLE and ZigBee signals. In BLE, the signal directly expresses information in frequency offsets, making the decoding quite straightforward, while in ZigBee, the offset quadrature phase shift keying is exactly equivalent to FSK, with the decoding similar to that in BLE. For phase shift keying systems such as 802.11b, the basic principle that a larger/smaller phase shift at a certain time corresponds to a higher/lower frequency offset still holds, thereby enabling decoding using our method. Thus, PassiveFSK can extract the frequency offset from ambient signals using biased filter tuning for mixer-free and low-power decoding. More importantly, no modification to hardware or software, nor any manipulation of raw bits in the physical layer at the transmitter, is required. We design a hardware prototype and leverage the processing pipeline of active receivers to develop a low-power decoding algorithm suitable for the tag. Extensive experimental results show that PssiveFSK direct decoding of commodity signals with a distance over 4 meters.

title: Thermal Conductance Characterization of Electrically Isolated Thermal Strap for Cryogenic Application
authors: Satomi Hasegawa, Masaya Hasegawa, Hiroki Akamatsu, Daisuke Kaneko, Ryota Takaku, Takuro Fujino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Superconducting transition-edge sensors are widely used in astronomical observations, such as those targeting the cosmic microwave background. The sensors are typically put on a low-temperature stage in a cryostat and cooled down by mechanical cryocoolers connected to the stage via thermal straps. Conventional thermal straps connect the stage to the cryocooler directly using metallic lines, causing unexpected electrical excess noise that potentially degrades instrument performance. To address this issue, we have developed a thermal strap in which a sapphire plate is sandwiched between high-purity copper plates to electrically isolate the stage from the cryocooler. We measured the thermal conductance of the fabricated sample at cryogenic temperatures and found that it reaches about 20W/K around 5K to 6K. This value is comparable to that of a pure copper strap, demonstrating its suitability for low-noise cryogenic applications.

title: Online Max-Min Fairness Guarantee for Request Completion Rates in Collaborative Edge Inference
authors: Jiale Huang, Yalan Wu, Jigang Wu, Jiaxin Wu, Siew-Kei Lam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: Recent endeavors on edge inference focus on system wide service performance optimization. However, they overlook fairness guarantee in request completion rates across users, which plays a pivotal role in enhancing user satisfaction. This paper fills this gap by formulating a long-term max-min user fairness problem in collaborative edge inference systems. The goal is to maximize the minimum request completion rate among users, by optimizing request offloading and resource allocation, under constraints of quality-of-service, resources, and time-coupled energy budgets. An online fairness guarantee algorithm is proposed to solve the formulated problem. The online algorithm transforms the problem into an equivalent maximization problem, and then decomposes it into a series of one-slot problems by decoupling time-coupled constraints. The online algorithm solves one-slot problem in dual domain, where the dual function is decomposed into request offloading problem and resource allocation problem. Agreedy strategy is proposed to solve request offloading problem in parallel, while convex optimization techniques are utilized to solve resource allocation problem in parallel. The asymptotic optimality of the online algorithm is proved. Experimental results show that the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline algorithms for most cases, in terms of minimum request completion rate, while consistently achieving near-perfect fairness.

title: Practical Lossless Volumetric Medical Image Compression via Tri-plane Context Tree Learning
authors: Yuanchao Bai, Yifan Zhao, Kai Wang, Yuanbo Du, Jie Cheng, Teng Fang, Xianming Liu, Wen Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Lossless compression of volumetric medical images is of paramount importance for clinical and research applications where data fidelity is essential. Traditional compression methods are often limited in efficiency due to rigid, handcrafted models. Conversely, deep neural network (DNN)-based compression methods, while effective, demand substantial computational resources, hindering deployment in resource-constrained settings. To address these challenges, we propose a novel tri-plane context tree (TCT)-based method for lossless volumetric medical image compression that delivers high performance without relying on DNNs or external training data. To exploit intra-slice and interslice redundancies, we introduce a compact tri-plane context representation that decomposes complex 3D context modeling into efficient 2D modeling on three orthogonal planes. By integrating this representation with a context tree framework, we develop an input-specific TCT model employing an adaptive binary tree structure. At each tree node, the model dynamically selects from a suite of tri-plane based predictors and contextual feature extractors, enabling data-adaptive context modeling tailored to local structural characteristics. Instead of offline training, we sample a subset of the input volume to learn the TCT model by optimizing the minimum description length (MDL) through iterative construction and pruning. With the learned TCT model, each pixel retrieves its corresponding context, computes the prediction residual using the predictor dictated by the context, and performs entropy encoding based on the associated histograms. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves compression performance on par with recent DNN-based methods on multiple datasets, while maintaining low computational cost and fast coding speeds, making it highly applicable in practice.
