title: Feasibility Study of a Sensorized Prosthetic Foot for Gait Monitoring and Prosthesis Control
authors: Alessandro Mazzarini, Ilaria Fagioli, Filippo Dell’Agnello, Fabrizio Moncelli, Emanuele Gruppioni, Nicola Vitiello, Emilio Trigili, Simona Crea
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The assessment of gait parameters in daily life offers valuable insights for tailoring rehabilitation protocols and personalizing lower-limb assistive devices. Integrating sensory systems into prostheses may enable adaptive control and continuous gait monitoring. Traditional assessment methods such as motion capture, force platforms, and wearable Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are effective but often lack seamless integration with prostheses, limiting their use in everyday environments. This article presents a novel plantar sensory system designed for integration with lower-limb prostheses. The system employs optoelectronic plantar pressure sensors embedded between the prosthetic foot and cosmetic cover at load-bearing regions of the gait cycle. Endurance testing confirmed its robustness over prolonged use. Ten healthy subjects participated in a study evaluating the system’s ability to estimate vertical ground reaction force (vGRF) during walking. A Gaussian Process Regressor was trained on sensory signals with force plate data as ground truth. Using leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, the model showed good generalizability (R² = 0.94 ± 0.04), high correlation with force platform data (Pearson r = 0.94 (0.05)), and comparable peak vGRF values. Model stability was assessed by repeating the training 30 times, yielding a coefficient of variation of the RMSE equal to 7.7%. The trained model was also applied to unseen data from two subjects with transtibial amputation, producing physiologically consistent vGRF profiles. The system also promptly detected gait events, identifying heel strike and toe off with median delays of 0.01 s and 0.002 s, respectively. These results support the potential of the system for gait parameter estimation in prosthetic applications.

title: Demand-Aware Multi-Area Multi-UAV Empowered Mobile Edge Computing: A Joint Energy and Delay Optimization_supp1-3697839.pdf
authors: Chaoda Peng
publish: N/A
abstract: Multi-UAV-assisted mobile edge computing (MEC) has emerged as a promising paradigm for smart city infrastructures, where multiple uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) collaboratively provide airborne computing services by bringing computational resources closer to end users, thereby reducing both communication costs and service latency. Existing multi-UAV-assisted MEC approaches typically employ fixed allocation schemes that predetermine the number of UAVs for a service area, failing to adapt to heterogeneous regional demands and leaving substantial UAV fleet capacity underutilized. To address this issue, we consider a multi-area multi-UAV-assisted MEC scenario where the control center jointly optimizes UAV deployment, user association, and resource allocation for each service area based on actual regional demands. This optimization enables multiple UAVs to be allocated to high-demand areas while assigning fewer UAVs to low-demand areas, thereby maximizing the service capacity of the entire UAV fleet. We formulate the optimization problem as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem (CMOP) that simultaneously minimizes the total system energy consumption and average user task completion delay, subject to deployment, user association, resource capacity, and task completion delay constraints. To effectively solve this CMOP, we propose a constrained multi-objective evolutionary algorithm that systematically reconstructs infeasible solutions into feasible ones through a constraint-guided solution reconstruction mechanism, thereby accelerating convergence toward feasible regions. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms five state-of-the-art baseline methods in solution diversity and convergence performance. The results validate the effectiveness of flexible UAV allocation strategies, establishing a foundation for demand-aware resource provisioning in multi-UAV-assisted MEC systems.

title: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence Information for Authors
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence

title: Special Issue on Future Mobile Core
authors: Rui Luis Aguiar, Periklis Chatzimisios, Namseok Ko, Xu Xia, Huanzhuo Wu
publish: IEEE Communications Standards Magazine
abstract: This editorial introduces the Special Issue on the Future Mobile Core. In the transition towards beyond-5G and 6G systems, the mobile core network becomes a central architectural enabler whose importance has traditionally been underemphasized relative to the radio access network. This editorial introduces the selected papers for this Special Issue, highlighting how softwarization, intelligence and cloud-native design, together with standardized data analytics and exposure functions, position the core as the natural locus for closed-loop automation and AI-assisted operation, while also introducing new challenges related to determinism, state management, security, and operational governance. By framing the core as both a programmable platform and a trustworthy control system, the editorial positions the contributions in the Special Issue, which collectively explore architectural redesign, protocol evolution, orchestration, Al integration, trust, and migration strategies as key research directions for realizing future mobile networks.

title: Model Free Adaptive Q-Learning for Stochastic Differential Games with State-Dependent Perturbations and Input Saturation
authors: Zhifu Jia, Na Xie
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper proposes an online adaptive Q-learning algorithm for linear-quadratic nonzero-sum stochastic differential games with completely unknown system dynamics, state-dependent perturbations, and input saturation constraints. A dual critic-actor network structure is developed for each player to reduce the Q-function estimation variance caused by stochastic noise, where the weighted fusion of two parallel critic networks and a projection-based actor network are designed for variance reduction and constraint satisfaction, respectively. An adaptive learning rate strategy is designed based on the temporal-difference error and gradient norm to balance the convergence speed and stability of the learning system. By constructing a composite stochastic Lyapunov function and combining the linear growth condition of perturbations, the uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB) of the system state and neural network weight estimation errors is proven in the mean-square sense. An autonomous driving game with two players is presented to verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method, and comparative results show that the algorithm outperforms fixed-learning-rate Q-learning and rule-based PID control in convergence speed, tracking accuracy and constraint satisfaction.

title: Event-Triggered Adaptive Enhanced Fast Terminal Sliding Mode Control Based on K ∞ Functions for Quadrotor UAVs
authors: Wendong Li, Shanping Wang, Haicheng Wan, Yujuan Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper proposes an event-triggered adaptive sliding mode control scheme for practical finite-time tracking of quadrotor position and attitude in the presence of perturbations. The core of the method is an enhanced fast terminal sliding mode (EFTSM) manifold, which improves convergence performance and ensures practical finite-time stability. By combining EFTSM with the super-twisting algorithm (STA), control chattering is effectively reduced. In addition, a class-K∞ function is introduced for STAgain scheduling. The resulting gain adaptation responds to disturbance variations without requiring prior knowledge of their upper bounds, thereby avoiding excessively high gains and smoothing the control effort. To reduce computation and communication burden in implementation, an event-triggered mechanism is adopted so that the control input is updated only at triggering instants. Simulation results based on quadrotor parameters demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in comparison with existing approaches.

title: TSF-Net: Balancing Long-Term Stability and Short-Term Agility in Stock Return Prediction
authors: Lutao Zheng, Guanjun Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Despite significant advances in machine learning for financial forecasting, prevailing approaches face a fundamental modeling challenge in simultaneously capturing long-term stability and short-term agility in stock return prediction. Current methodologies tend to treat these temporal characteristics as mutually exclusive, where models demonstrate strong performance in identifying persistent patterns, they often suppress short-term fluctuations as noise, thus lacking sensitivity to short-term market fluctuations. Conversely, models that overemphasize short-term fluctuations risk overfitting to transient market noise, thereby undermining long-term robustness. To resolve this trade-off, we propose the temporal stability-flexibility network (TSF-Net), which addresses the limitations of existing architectures that rely on single-module processing or fixed-weight fusion of mixed-frequency features. Unlike these approaches, which inevitably sacrifice either trend stability or noise sensitivity, TSF-Net introduces an adaptive weight allocation strategy that dynamically reconciles long-term trends with short-term fluctuations based on real-time volatility regimes. The framework first decouples temporal regimes via wavelet decomposition, then processes purified components through parallel, noninterfering pathways (stability-oriented transformer for low-frequency fundamentals; agility-oriented TCN with cross-stock attention), and finally integrates them through learnable gating mechanisms that optimally balance persistent patterns against transient shocks. On 1043 Chinese A-share stocks (2013–2022), TSF-Net achieves Sharpe ratios of 0.75–1.69, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 44%–163% with 20%–88% lower maximum drawdown. Ablation studies confirm 213% volatility increase without frequency separation and 62.5% return degradation without real-time dynamics capture.

title: Zero-Axis Control Based Battery Impedance Identification in Series-end Winding Motor Drives
authors: Dong Jiang, Ding Luo, Hongyan Qu, Boyang Li, Min Zhou, Tao Cai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: As one of the core components of the Electric Powertrain Subsystems in electric vehicles (EV), the efficient operation of power batteries serves as an essential prerequisite for normal EV operation, thereby highlighting the critical importance of battery status management. The AC impedance method, as an efficient and non-destructive battery state monitoring technique, can be integrated with the traction motor drive in EV. Compared with the widely used half-bridge voltage source inverter for traction drive, the series-end winding topology demonstrates advantages including much high output voltage capability while exhibiting significant cost advantages over the full-bridge topology for traction drive applications. This study therefore adopts the series-end winding topology and establishes an impedance identification framework based on zero-axis control degrees of freedom. Through coordinated regulation of zero-axis and q-axis disturbance currents, an amplitude-frequency transfer model is constructed between AC-side disturbance signals and DC-side response current. This approach achieves simultaneous high-precision extraction of battery impedance and dynamic compensation of torque ripple without additional hardware. Experimental validation was conducted using a self-developed series-end winding topology prototype, with impedance spectra measured across 1Hz-1000 Hz frequency domains, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed methodology in series-winding applications.

title:
WSe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    and PCBM Type II Planar Heterojunction for Flexible Broadband Photodetector Application
authors: Tulika Bajpai, Shweta Tripathi
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: The opto-electrical properties and the performance of the affordable Al/PCBM/WSe2/PEDOT: PSS/ITO/PET structured photodetector is analysed in this research article. The n-type organic semiconductor PCBM forms a type II planar P-N heterojunction with p-type inorganic semiconductor WSe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> to function as the active layer for the proposed device. The PEDOT: PSS deposited over flexible ITO coated PET substrate serves as hole transport layer (HTL) to ensure efficient hole transmission, thus helps in improving the device performance. The device's performance is noted to be excellent in the ultraviolet (UV), visible and near infrared (NIR) spectrum. High external quantum efficiency (EQE) is the outcome of photomultiplication process in the carrier transport mechanism. High EQEs and Responsivity (RS) at 300 nm (UV), 450 nm (visible) and 800 nm (NIR) for a +1V bias are 120.53×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>% and 2916 A/W, 85.48×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>% and 3102 A/W, 31.85×104% and 2055 A/W respectively. This study highlights a promising approach for developing efficient, flexible, and cost-effective optoelectronic devices.

title: Human-in-the-loop Control Framework for Robot-mediated Error Augmentation Training Based on Muscle Synergy Assessment
authors: Xiao Li, Aiguo Song, Jianwei Lai, Wenbin Zhang, Kui Qian, Junhui Wu, Ying Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Robot-mediated error augmentation (EA) offers a promising paradigm for enhancing motor adaptation by amplifying movement errors rather than compensating for them. However, integrating EA into human-in-the-loop (HITL) control systems remains challenging. This difficulty arises from the lack of performance metrics that reflect neuromuscular adaptation and the limited ability to efficiently adapt control parameters to user-specific motor strategies. To address these challenges, we propose a HITL control framework that leverages muscle synergy analysis to guide the personalization of EA intensity. A synergy-based performance metric was designed to quantify motor adaptation by measuring structural similarity across muscle synergies. This metric served as the optimization objective for a Gaussian process-based Bayesian algorithm, which adaptively tuned the EA gain based on individual responses. An 8-day study involving ten healthy subjects performing non-dominant limb reaching tasks validated the proposed framework. The experimental group achieved significantly greater reductions in trajectory deviation than the control group, alongside robust within-group improvements (p < 0.01) and positive inter-group trends in muscle synergy similarities (gw,gc). These results demonstrate reliable convergence of the optimization process, robust inter-subject adaptability, and the effectiveness of the synergy metric in capturing adaptation. Overall, the findings highlight the potential of the proposed framework as a foundational tool for adaptive motor training in human–robot interaction systems.

title: Self-supervised Representation Learning for Dynamic Functional Connectivity with Subject-wise and Temporal Contrasts
authors: Jianfei Zhu, Lijun An, Haiqi Zhu, Feng Jiang, Shaohui Liu, Baichun Wei, Chunzhi Yi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Dynamic functional connectivity (dFC) captures temporal dynamics in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to better characterize brain activity, supporting multiple downstream analyses. However, effective modeling strategies for extracting representative signals from dFC remain underexplored. We proposed a novel Subject-wise and Temporal Contrastive Transformer (STCT) framework that advances dynamic connectivity modeling through a dual-constraint contrastive learning strategy. Our STCT framework uniquely integrates two objectives: (1) subject-wise contrast to preserve inter-individual specificity, and (2) temporal contrast to capture dynamic dependencies, which is a critical dimension overlooked in prior methods. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrated superior performance of STCT over both supervised and self-supervised approaches based only on subject-wise contrast in various predictive domains spanning demographics, cognition, and mental disorder diagnosis. Inter-pretability analysis further revealed lateralized connectivity patterns in Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) classification, suggesting the potential of STCT to highlight clinically relevant connectivity patterns.

title: Randomization Mechanism Based on Elliptical Distributions for Location Privacy in Geo-Indistinguishability
authors: Dan Yu, Xiufang Shi, Li Chai, Mincheng Wu, Jiming Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Geo-indistinguishability (GI), as a formal notion of location privacy, is implemented by perturbing the users' exact locations using random noise. For location-sensitive scenarios, e.g., localization and navigation, the introduction of random noise would decrease the data utility. In this paper, we investigate alternative randomization mechanisms that can provide higher data utility than the commonly used planar Laplacian mechanism while keeping the same privacy performance. Specifically, we investigate the usage of elliptical distributions to increase data utility. Firstly, we derive two sufficient and necessary conditions to determine whether a randomization mechanism satisfies GI. Using these conditions, we verify the feasibility of elliptical distributions for achieving GI and find that, except for the planar Laplacian, the Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> distribution can also satisfy GI. Secondly, we adopt the Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> distribution into the randomization mechanism to achieve GI and quantify its performance in terms of privacy level and data utility. To optimize the data utility and privacy level of Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> distribution in practical applications, we formulate two optimization problems: one aims to maximize data utility under a given privacy level, and the other aims to maximize the privacy guarantee under the constraint of data utility. Extensive experiments have been conducted in two typical location-based applications, including fingerprint-based indoor localization and location-based advertising. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results evaluate the performance of the Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> distribution and provide insight into the selection of randomization mechanism under different scenarios by comparing Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> mechanism with the Laplacian one.

title: Phenyl-Based Intrinsic Modification for Enhanced Interfacial Reliability of Silicone Gel in High Voltage Power Module Packaging
authors: Dongxin He, Xiangze An, Keming Liu, Cong Zhang, Yunhao Li, Hongshun Liu, Qingquan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: High voltage and high power density development of power modules challenges the reliability of packaging. Packaging silicone gel is prone to interface delamination failure due to its poor intrinsic adhesion performance. In order to solve the problem of early failure caused by insufficient interface adhesion in the packaging of power modules from the perspective of the material itself, this study starts from the intrinsic structure modification and prepares phenyl silicone gel by introducing phenyl. The influence of phenyl content on material properties is systematically studied by constructing a test structure that simulates the interface characteristics of encapsulation and layer substrate. The results shows that when the phenyl content reaches 25%-30%, the π-metal interaction increases the adhesion strength by more than 30%, and the failure mode shifts to cohesive failure. This system with higher phenyl content exhibits excellent adhesion retention and thermal stability after high temperature aging, with the lowest increase in leakage current and great maintenance of breakdown field strength. This study significantly improves the interface integrity of silicone gel under thermal mechanical stress, reduces the leakage risk caused by interface defects and provides an effective solution for the packaging of high reliability power modules.

title: A Study on an Outer-Rotor Hybrid Motor Considering for High Power Density
authors: Ye-Seo Lee, Taeho Ha, Joon Phil Choi, Won-Ho Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: In recent electrification applications, including automotive drive systems, the demand for high-power-density permanent magnet motors capable of delivering high torque and power within a limited volume has been increasing. In this paper, a hybrid-type permanent magnet synchronous motor (H-PMSM), which can simultaneously utilize the advantages of a radial flux motor (RFM) and an axial flux motor (AFM), is proposed, and an outer-rotor structure is adopted to improve winding manufacturability and the slot fill factor. The proposed outer-rotor H-PMSM is configured with the AFM in the upper part and the RFM in the lower part, and the two parts are connected by a single winding so that the conventional end-turn region can act as an additional effective conductor. In addition, by adopting the outer-rotor structure, the ease of the winding process is improved and a high slot fill factor can be achieved. The proposed structure was analyzed by finite element analysis (FEA), and it was confirmed that the total torque was greater than the simple sum of the torques generated by the AFM part and the RFM part, indicating the possibility of an additional torque contribution from the end turn. In addition, a prototype motor was fabricated, and it was confirmed that the slot fill factor of the outer-rotor structure was improved to approximately 40% compared with that of the inner-rotor structure. It was also confirmed that the FEA results agreed well with the prototype results in terms of performance, thereby validating the proposed outer-rotor H-PMSM.

title: A Sliced Learning Framework for Online Disturbance Identification in Quadrotor SO(3) Attitude Control_supp1-3689054.pdf
authors: Akiya KAMIMURA
publish: N/A
abstract: This article introduces a dimension-decomposed geometric learning framework called Sliced Learning for disturbance identification in quadrotor geometric attitude control. Instead of conventional learning-from-states, this framework adopts a learning-from-error strategy by using the Lie-algebraic error representation as the input feature, enabling axiswise space-decomposition (“slicing”) while preserving the SO(3) structure. This is highly consistent with the geometric mechanism of cognitive control observed in neuroscience, where neural systems organize adaptive representations within structured subspaces to enable cognitive flexibility and efficiency. Based on this framework, we develop a lightweight and structurally interpretable Sliced Adaptive-Neuro Mapping module. The high-dimensional mapping for online identification is axially “sliced” into multiple low-dimensional submappings (“slices”), implemented by shallow neural networks and adaptive laws. These neural networks and adaptive laws are updated online via Lyapunov-based adaptation within their respective shared subspaces. To enhance interpretability, we prove exponential convergence despite time-varying disturbances and inertia uncertainties. To the best of the authors' knowledge, sliced learning is among the first frameworks to demonstrate lightweight online neural adaptation at 400 Hz on resource-constrained microcontroller units, such as STM32, with real-world experimental validation.

title: Robust Low-Rank Sparse Framework for Video-Based Affective Computing
authors: Feng-Qi Cui, Jinyang Huang, Sirui Zhao, Xinyu Li, Xin Yan, Ziyu Jia, Xiaokang Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Video-based Affective Computing (VAC) is central to emotion understanding and emerging consumer electronics applications, yet it often suffers from unstable optimization and representational degradation under complex, non-stationary affective dynamics in the wild. A key challenge is that short-term behavioral fluctuations can convey different affective meanings under different long-term emotional contexts, whereas most existing VAC models entangle heterogeneous factors in a single latent space, leading to component mixing and cross-scale inconsistency. We propose the Low-Rank Sparse Emotion Understanding Framework (LSEF), a plug-and-play framework grounded in the low-rank sparse principle that models affective dynamics as the composition of long-term emotional bases and sparse transient perturbations. Specifically, LSEF consists of four modules: the Stability Encoding Module (SEM) extracts low-rank bases by emphasizing slow-varying context patterns; the Dynamic Decoupling Module (DDM) isolates sparse transients via temporal routing and orthogonalized relational propagation; and the Consistency Integration Module (CIM) restores multi-scale coherence between stability and reactivity under hierarchical aggregation. We further propose a Rank Aware Optimization (RAO) strategy that adaptively modulates perturbation strength according to rank- and sparsity-sensitive structure to improve training stability and generalization. Experiments on discrete and continuous VAC benchmarks show consistent gains in robustness and dynamic discrimination across general CNN-based backbones, validating hierarchical low-rank sparse modeling as a principled foundation for video affect understanding.

title: Nezha-FX: A Morphing HAUV Designed Based on Balanced Cross-Domain Performance Optimization_supp3-3691061.mp4
authors: Hexiong Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: As emerging multirotor-type hybrid aerial underwater vehicles capable of both flight and underwater operations, numerous conceptual prototypes have been proposed in recent years. However, existing designs lack a balanced consideration of cross-medium stability, underwater maneuverability, and mode-switching reliability within a single configuration, and there remains no unified evaluation criterion for cross-domain performance. To compensate for the limitations of conventional optimization-based design, this work leverages key constraints identified through experiments and employ a multiobjective optimization approach to achieve a balanced design among these competing requirements. A lightweight and reliable foldable-arm system is further developed to support the implementation of the optimized configuration. In addition, through systematic integration of aerial propulsion bench test data with vehicle design parameters, we develop novel static quantitative metrics (cross-domain thrust margin@50%) to comprehensively assess overall design rationality. Finally, simulation and field tests show that the underwater damping of the final prototype has decreased by 55.2%. Compared with the previous generation, the cross-domain average power consumption is reduced by 68.9%.

title: Grid-Aligned Particle Swarm for Large-Scale Multiobjective Optimization
authors: Juan Zou, Jiajun Peng, Hui Bai, Jin Liu, Ran Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Large-scale multiobjective optimization problems (LSMOPs) involve high-dimensional decision spaces that challenge algorithmic convergence and diversity. Existing Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) variants often suffer from misaligned search directions and redundant exploration in these high-dimensional settings. To address these limitations, we propose Grid-Aligned Particle Swarm (GAPS) framework, which integrates two complementary mechanisms. First, at the micro level, search direction reconstruction realigns particle velocities toward promising solutions. This mechanism provides explicit directional correction, which is absent in standard PSO updates. Second, at the macro level, adaptive grid decomposition progressively refines the decision space from coarse to fine grids. In contrast to static or reduction strategies, this approach avoids information loss and dynamically focuses exploration on promising regions without premature restriction. Extensive experiments on benchmarks and a real-world application demonstrate that GAPS achieves faster convergence and superior solution quality in comparison with state-of-the-art approaches.

title: A Robust Underwater Scheduled MAC Protocol Against Nodes Position Perturbation
authors: Mengqi Yu, Yong Jin, Qian Wei, Chengbing He, Jing Han
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In Underwater Acoustic Sensor Networks (UASNs), designing an efficient Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol is severely hindered by long propagation delays and unpredictable node positional perturbations caused by dynamic marine environments. Traditional scheduled MAC protocols rely on rigid, fixed-length time slots and pessimistic guard intervals to avoid collisions, which inevitably leads to substantial channel resource waste and underutilization. To address these challenges, given that node perturbations follow a Gaussian distribution, we embed prior statistical knowledge of these perturbations into the MAC scheduling model, and propose a chance-constrained Mixed-Integer Linear Programming algorithm to solve it, leading to the Non-Fixed Slot-Positional Perturbation Medium Access Control (NSPP-MAC) protocol. Because it models the MAC scheduling problem by integrating key information related to collision-free packet reception—including data propagation delays, packet lengths, and node perturbations—the proposed NSPP-MAC can dynamically allocate transmission moments without packet reception collisions. Extensive simulations demonstrate that NSPP-MAC comprehensively outperforms conventional modified TDMA and Polling protocols. It significantly reduces average end-to-end latency and packet inter-reception intervals while maximizing effective network throughput, providing a highly robust, scalable, and efficient solution for complex Underwater Internet of Things (IoUT) applications.

title: Direct Parameter Mapping Optimization Control of LCC Resonant Converter for Distributed Photovoltaics
authors: Yun Xu, Wenjing Wang, Kaiyin Mo, Peng Yang, Zhangke Yan, Linhai Hu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: With the advancement of DC distributed photo-voltaic systems, there is an increasing demand for DC converters with high performance in terms of wide gain and power range, high conversion ratio, and high efficiency. The LCC resonant converter with variable-frequency and phase-shift modulation (VFPSM) shows great application potential due to its advantages of wide soft-switching range, high gain ratio, and high efficiency.However, the existing VFPSM schemes are limited by model accuracy and the generality of control strategies, lacking a universal and efficient control framework that can directly map any steady-state operating target to switching frequency and phase shift angle.To address the above problems, this paper proposes a high-precision analytical model based on VFPSM and a direct parameter mapping universal control framework. Centered on this framework, an optimized variable-frequency and phase-shift modulation (OVFPSM) strategy is developed, which can accurately maintain critical soft switching and minimize reactive power across the entire input range, thereby significantly improving efficiency. The proposed method and model are thoroughly validated through a 500W LCC resonant converter prototype.The experimental results show that the model has a prediction error of less than 1.5% for the switching frequency and phase shift angle. The proposed control strategy can maintain critical Zero Voltage Switching (ZVS) across a wide input range, significantly reducing reactive power. The efficiency under heavy/light load is improved by 3.3%/4.5% and 2.5%/2% compared to traditional variable frequency modulation and VFPSM, respectively.

title: Experiment of an Active Phased Array System Based on a Four-Beam Integrated Traveling Wave Tube in W-Band
authors: Ran Sun, Jinsheng Yang, Huanli Ji, Hanshuo Mu, Pan Pan, Jun Cai, Jinjun Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: This article reports the research progress on a four-beam planar-integrated traveling wave tube (TWT) in the W-band for active phased array (APA) system. Based on low-voltage, high-frequency, and high-transmission-rate electron optical systems, a four-beam integrated TWT has been developed and tested. The experimental results show that each channel achieved over 93% dynamic beam transmission at a 1% duty cycle. Each of the four beams delivered more than 13 W of output power with a gain exceeding 30 dB across a 4 GHz bandwidth. The overall tube efficiency is 7.29%. Comprehensive characterization demonstrates amplitude-phase consistency within <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 0.9$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB in output power, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 4.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB in gain, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ \pm 11.2^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in phase, while also providing phase-adjustability capability. Under synchronized excitation signals and pulse control, an APA system based on the integrated TWT was built. The system demonstrates effective spatial power combining and stable directional control, achieving a combined output power of 21.9 W with a combining efficiency of 39.2%. Furthermore, beam scanning over a range of ±5° is realized with a pointing resolution of 1°. These results establish the integrated TWT as a promising radiation source for radar tracking applications.

title: Development and Flight Trial of a UAV-based Gamma-Ray and Neutron Detection System for Large-Area Radioactivity Mapping and Source Activity Estimation
authors: Lysander Miller, Airlie Chapman, James Kennedy, Richard Hebden, Jeremy M. C. Brown
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science
abstract: Advances in scintillation crystal and Silicon Photo-Multiplier (SiPM) technologies have enabled the development of compact, lightweight, and low-power radiation detectors that are suitable for integration with Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). This integration enables efficient and cost-effective large-area radiation monitoring while minimising occupational exposure. In this work, a SiPM-based NaIL scintillation detection payload was developed, characterised, and mounted on a multirotor UAV for ground-level radioactivity mapping, gamma-ray and neutron source localisation, and activity estimation applications. To support these capabilities, an analytic radionuclide detection efficiency model was developed and used to estimate radioactivity on the ground from aerial energy spectrum measurements. The analytic expression for the detection efficiency incorporated physical phenomena, including the branching ratio, detector solid angle, air attenuation, and intrinsic peak efficiency, leading to agreement within 10% of experimental radionuclide detection efficiencies. The UAV-based radiation detection system was physically validated through a controlled indoor live radioactive source demonstration at 1.5 m, 3 m, and 4.5 m flight heights. Using the developed ground-level radioactivity estimation method, <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">137</sup>Cs and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">60</sup>Co sources were successfully localised within 0.5 m, and their activities were estimated with errors on the order of 10% or less.

title: Artificial Micro-Doppler Flashes for Aerial Vehicle Identification: Drone Vehicle Identification Number via Blade Coding
authors: Andrey Sheleg, Dmytro Vovchuk, Sergey Geyman, Amir Boag, Aviel Glam, Pavel Ginzburg
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: The growing deployment of aerial vehicles across various domains, including unmanned drone delivery and urban air mobility, demands robust identification mechanisms capable of adapting to a rapidly evolving landscape, especially within congested airspace and urban environments. Existing identification systems primarily rely on visual tracking or wireless communication, each with inherent limitations: the former is constrained by line-of-sight and environmental conditions, while the latter depends on continuous connectivity and GPS data, making it vulnerable to spoofing. To address these challenges, we propose a novel radar-based identification method that introduces artificial micro-Doppler flashes generated by coded scatterers mounted on rotor blades. This approach creates unique, repeatable Doppler signatures, analogous to a Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), or Aerial VIN (AVIN), that can be interrogated by standard surveillance radars. Through a combination of theoretical modeling and experimental validation in a controlled indoor environment, we demonstrate the generation and detection of structured micro-Doppler signatures with high signal-to-noise contrast. The resulting code space reliably supports more than 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">64</sup> uniquely distinguishable configurations, excluding axisymmetric redundancies, thereby confirming the feasibility of micro-Doppler-based identification for aerial platforms. Extrapolation to outdoor scenarios indicates a feasible detection range of up to 30 km under typical X-band radar conditions. These results suggest a promising path toward secure, passive identification of aerial vehicles, eliminating the need for onboard power or communication modules, and providing a reliable alternative in case of system failure.

title: A 40k fps 99.5 dB Wide Dynamic Range PWM Image Sensor Featuring a Low-Power Pixel Array_supp1-3695072.avi
authors: Kaiming Nie
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper presents a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$500\times 1000$</tex-math> </inline-formula> array pulse-width modulation (PWM) sensor with wide dynamic range, low power consumption fabricated in 110-nm CIS FSI process. The prototype demonstrates key performance metrics with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$17\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m pixel pitch, achieving 40k fps full-resolution imaging while maintaining 21.5% fill factor and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20.40\times 11.08$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> die size. The chip achieves 99.5 dB dynamic range while maintaining 37.5 pJ/pixel<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>frame energy efficiency under dark conditions. Dynamic power management scheme implements adaptive row activation achieving 0.75W dark-current power consumption. By encoding scene information in compressed bit-stream format, the sensor provides high spatiotemporal resolution while eliminating redundancy in raw data transmission. Quantizing light intensity directly in the temporal domain, instead of the voltage domain, enables a wide dynamic range to be achieved at a substantially lower power cost. This parallelized architecture shows strong potential for intelligent vision systems requiring real-time high-speed processing capabilities.

title: Cost-Effective Co-Optimization of SoC Partitioning and Global Chiplet Placement for 2.5D Integration
authors: Chou-Yen-Chih Li, Chih-Yu Li, Yung-Chih Chen, Liang-Chia Cheng
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: As System-on-Chip (SoC) designs grow in complexity and size, fabricating monolithic dies becomes increasingly costly, making chiplet-based integration a promising alternative. However, existing partitioning methods often produce sub-optimal results due to inaccurate estimation of design quality, especially when physical information such as chiplet shape is unavailable. To address this challenge, we propose a chiplet partitioning and placement co-optimization flow that integrates placement into the partitioning stage, enabling more accurate evaluation of power, latency, and recurring engineering cost. By performing concurrent placement and chiplet shape adjustment during partitioning, our method overcomes the lack of early physical information and achieves more accurate quality estimation and superior overall design quality, improving final design quality by up to $37.87 \%$ with an average improvement of $\mathbf{1 3. 5 6 \%}$ compared to conventional two-stage flows.

title: Semantic-Guided Test Generation using Fine-Tuned LLMs for Validation of Hardware Accelerators
authors: Emma Andrews, Aruna Jayasena, Prabhat Mishra
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: The increasing complexity and heterogeneity of programmable hardware accelerators, such as Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and Tensor Processing Units (TPU), pose a significant challenge for automated test generation and functional validation. Traditional validation techniques often struggle to scale with architectural diversity and cannot effectively exploit the semantic relationships between instructions and data. Validation using large language models (LLMs) is a promising avenue for generating assembly programs (test vectors) for processor verification since LLMs are trained with diverse general-purpose processor designs. Unfortunately, LLMs are unsuitable for validation of programmable hardware accelerators since there is a lack of training data for such implementations. In this paper, we propose an automated framework that fine-tunes LLMs to generate semantically correct test cases directed toward improved design coverage while monitoring the functional correctness of the outputs. The generated test cases are evaluated by a compiler for correctness before using them for validation of hardware accelerators. We facilitate a mechanism for the LLM to observe the design coverage on the implementation based on the previously generated test patterns. Extensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our framework can achieve 33% improvement in design coverage compared to state-of-the-art test generation with the added advantage of monitoring the functional correctness of the design. Our framework has identified several functional bugs in the open-source tiny-gpu implementation.

title: Synonym Knowledge Graph Enhanced Language Model for Inconsistent Hallucination Detection
authors: Jiacheng Yang, Hai-Tao Zheng, Hui Wang, Hong-Gee Kim
publish: ACM Transactions on Asian and Low-Resource Language Information Processing
abstract: None

title: Mix-MoE: Improving Multilingual Machine Translation of Large Language Models through Mixed MoEs
authors: Bo Li, Tianyu Dong, Shaolin Zhu, Deyi Xiong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great promise in multilingual machine translation (MT), even with limited bilingual supervision. However, fine-tuning LLMs with parallel corpora presents major challenges, namely parameter interference. To address these issues, we propose Mix-MoE, a mixed Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to train LLMs for multilingual MT. Our framework operates in two distinct stages: (1) post-pretraining with MoE on monolingual corpora, and (2) post-pretraining with MoE on parallel corpora. Crucially, we divide the MoE layers into two specialized groups: Language Model Experts (LM Experts) and Machine Translation Experts (MT Experts). LM Experts are designed to capture and retain the monolingual knowledge learned by the pre-trained LLM. MT Experts, on the other hand, are specifically trained to acquire and store bilingual translation knowledge. Furthermore, to facilitate effective interaction between these specialized experts and leverage potential underlying structural patterns in text, we introduce a routing mechanism enhanced by Fourier Transform features derived from model representations. The experimental results demonstrate that Mix-MoE excels in multilingual MT, significantly outperforming existing baselines and showing notable progress in mitigating parameter interference.

title: Far-end Reconstruction-Guided Separation-based Acoustic Echo Cancellation with Flow Matching
authors: Gyeong-Su Kim, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) suppresses echo in a microphone signal while preserving near-end speech. Most neural AEC systems directly predict the near-end speech. We propose a framework that separates the representation into near-end and echo streams and adds a far-end reconstructor using flow matching that leverages the estimated echo. The backbone operates in real-time and comprises three stages-fusion, split, and reconstruction: it fuses the microphone input with a far-end reference, splits features into near-end and echo streams, and reconstructs both using source-aware skip connections and cross-stream interaction. The reconstructor is trained via conditional flow matching to recover a time-aligned far-end reference and is used only during training, so inference introduces no additional parameters or latency. We further introduce far-end reconstruction (FR)-guided self-supervised pretraining, which needs no near-end or echo labels and provides label-free, indirect guidance to the echo stream via reconstruction. On a customized dataset with diverse nonlinearities and delays and on the AEC Challenge blind test set, our method outperforms strong baselines, indicating that two-stream modeling plus FR-based guidance improves robustness to echo variability while preserving near-end quality.

title: Combined Integral and Fractional Carrier Frequency Offset Estimation in OFDM
authors: Fadi Jerji, Ivan Roberto Santana Casella, Cristiano Akamine
publish: IEEE Transactions on Broadcasting
abstract: This paper presents the mathematical analysis and simulation results for a time-domain Combined Integral and Fractional Estimator (CIFE) for the Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO) of Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) communication systems. The proposed method is based on manipulating the number of null subcarriers in a single OFDM preamble symbol, instead of using duplicate symbols as required by most methods found in the literature. The results obtained, including the analysis of the Advanced Television Systems Committee 3.0 (ATSC 3.0) bootstrap, recently adopted by Brazilian TV 3.0, showed that the fractional and integer values of CFO can be accurately estimated in the range of multiple SubCarrier Spacing (SCS), even in low Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR) scenarios. The proposed method can be considered a generalization of the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Schmidl and Cox Estimation (SCE) methods. Furthermore, the proposed symbol structure reduces the preamble overhead while remaining adequate for time synchronization and frame detection.

title: A Reinforcement-Conditioned Diffusion Model for Electromagnetic Wave Morphology Manipulation of Moving Object Inside Complex Media
authors: Jiahui Wang, Da Li, Jinyan Ma, Xiaokai Bai, Ruifeng Li, Pedro Cheong, Wai-Wa Choi, Er-Ping Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Achieving flexible manipulation of specific electromagnetic wave morphology has become an indispensable technique for numerous promising applications, such as wireless communication, energy harvesting, and parameter estimation. However, how to realize efficient and noninvasive electromagnetic wave manipulation for moving objects within complex media remains a significant challenge due to the complicated disordered scattering characteristics. To address this problem, this article proposes a manipulation methodology based on a novel reinforcement-conditioned diffusion model (RC-DM). Specifically, the generalized Wigner–Smith (GWS) operator is leveraged to achieve remarkable target-centric field enhancement or spatial suppression for the desired object inside complex scattering media. Subsequently, to enable noninvasive and accurate wave morphology manipulation for moving objects, the RC-DM is proposed to form the desired electromagnetic field solely through the utilization of the system scattering matrix. Furthermore, an improved sampling process of RC-DM is proposed, offering enhanced efficiency and accuracy to realize a near-real-time response of wave manipulation for moving objects. Finally, the corresponding versatility and performance are comprehensively demonstrated through the numerical validation and experiment under different moving trajectories. The results show that our proposed methodology has excellent effectiveness and robustness to address the challenge of electromagnetic wave morphology manipulation for moving objects inside complex scattering media.

authors: N/A

title: CO-NAS: Joint Neural Architecture Search for Compression, Quantization, and Edge–Cloud Model Offloading
authors: Eiraj Saqib, Oscar Artur Bernd Berg, Isaac Sánchez Leal, Irida Shallari, Silvia Krug, Axel Jantsch, Mattias O’nils
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The efficient deployment of deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource-constrained edge devices necessitates a delicate balance between model accuracy, communication bandwidth, and computational latency. While existing methods often address partitioning, quantization, or compression in isolation, this paper introduces CO-NAS (Joint Neural Architecture Search for Compression, Quantization, and Edge–Cloud Model Offloading), a unified framework that jointly optimizes these three critical dimensions for collaborative edge-cloud inference. By utilizing an adapted version of the stochastic architecture search (SNAS) paradigm, CO-NAS leverages Temperature-Annealed Softmax relaxations to enable gradient-based optimization over discrete choices of partition points, compression ratios, and activation bit-widths. Extensive experiments on ResNet-50 and VGG-16 across CIFAR-100 and Fashion-MNIST datasets demonstrate the framework’s efficacy. CO-NAS discovers Pareto-optimal configurations, such as a ResNet-50 model for CIFAR-100 achieving 70.1% accuracy with extreme compression (48× channel reduction and 1-bit quantization), representing a total compression of over 1500× on transmitted features. Crucially, exhaustive grid search validation confirms that CO-NAS reliably identifies top-performing architectures within the vast search space, while achieving a 21×-27× reduction in search cost compared to the exhaustive baseline. The results validate CO-NAS as a scalable and efficient approach for automating the co-design of accurate and communication-efficient neural networks for the edge-cloud paradigm.

title: Wildfire Occurrence Prediction via Modular Reuse of Frozen Intermediate Gemma 3-1B Decoder Blocks
authors: Ayoub Jadouli, Chaker El Amrani
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper studies whether frozen intermediate decoder blocks from a pretrained foundation model can be reused inside a lightweight tabular predictor for wildfire occurrence. The reused pretrained backbone comes from Gemma 3-1B [1]. We consider a Moroccan daily wildfire dataset composed of meteorological, vegetation, moisture, topographic, and lagged variables. A trainable adapter maps each 276-dimensional lag-augmented feature vector to the hidden size of Gemma 3-1B; two frozen intermediate decoder blocks (layers 8 and 9) are then applied to a single pseudo-token representation, followed by a small classifier head. This design should be interpreted as modular reuse of pretrained nonlinear transformations rather than as evidence that the language model encodes wildfire-specific physical dynamics. On the temporally held-out 2022 validation set, the proposed model attains a strong sensitivity-oriented trade-off, with recall 0.9433 and F<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> 0.8838, improving over standard feed-forward and convolutional baselines, while a much smaller FFN with positional encoding achieves the best overall balanced F<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> (0.8957) and AUC (0.9516). The results indicate that frozen intermediate blocks can be useful in recall-oriented environmental classification under limited-data conditions, but they do not outperform all simpler baselines. We therefore position the method as a modular transfer-learning alternative whose strengths, limitations, and deployment trade-offs must be reported explicitly.

title: Mutual Edge: A New Alternative beyond Private and Public Edge in IIoT Resource Provisioning
authors: Gahyeon Kwon, Kyungwoon Cho, Hyokyung Bahn
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper presents mutual edge, a new enterprise-driven edge resource provisioning paradigm for Industrial IoT systems, which addresses the limitations of both private and public edge models. Traditional resource provisioning often relies on overallocated private hardware or costly public edge rentals, which impose significant financial burdens. As an alternative, we propose the mutual edge model, where each industrial enterprise provisions a fraction of workloads using its own hardware and leases or lends resources across non-overlapping peak periods. To support this paradigm, we formulate a cost model that jointly considers hardware depreciation, electricity cost for each resource component, and leasing revenue from idle resources. We then formalize the problem as a constrained optimization, where the objective is to minimize execution cost while strictly meeting real-time task deadlines. Since this paradigm shift introduces challenges such as safe execution on mutually untrusted systems, we design and integrate security- and economics-aware mechanisms within a unified framework to address these challenges. Extensive simulations show that the proposed mutual edge framework reduces average computing cost by 78% and 56% compared to public and private edge models, respectively, under dynamic task sets and realistic IIoT scenarios.

title: Different Layers Models Based on Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for Computing Time-Varying Problems: A Survey
authors: Zanyu Tang, Hailu Wang, Jing Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) significantly enhance the representation and learning capabilities of complex dynamic systems by parameterising the derivatives of hidden states through neural networks. For the widely existing time-varying problems in reality, traditional methods, such as gradient neural network, suffer from insufficient real-time solving accuracy due to the failure to embed the differential structure of systems. Zeroing Neural Dynamics (ZND) models effectively improve both accuracy and real-time performance by constructing and zeroing error functions, transforming the solving problem into a convergence process of a dynamical system. These models can be mathematically categorized as a special class of NODEs. This survey presents a novel ZND classification framework, which characterizes the internal mathematical structure of models through derivative orders. This framework not only systematically organizes the evolutionary trajectory of ZND techniques from first-order to higher-order, and from single-layer to multi-layer models, but also reveals the intrinsic connection between model design and engineering requirements through examples such as robotic manipulator control. Finally, the survey further identifies future directions, aiming to provide a solid theoretical foundation and clear evolutionary pathway for subsequent research.

title: Cross-device Nonverbal-based Person Identification Using Mobile-sensed Breath Sounds
authors: Jung-Tsung Kuo, Van-Thuan Tran, Hsiao-Mei Lin, Yuh-Shyan Hwang, Wei-Ho Tsai
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Person identification using breath sounds has emerged as a promising biometric modality. While previous research demonstrated the feasibility of using stethoscopes or single mobile devices, the critical challenge of cross-device generalization remains unexplored. This work presents the investigation of cross-device person identification using mobile-sensed bronchial breath sounds. We collected a multi-device dataset comprising recordings from 20 subjects using three different mobile devices, with three sessions per subject per device. We propose a framework combining the Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) with supervised contrastive learning and dual data augmentation (traditional techniques and diffusion-based augmentation). Evaluation using device-based and session-based cross-validation reveals that cross-device scenarios are significantly more challenging than same-device scenarios (a 5-7% gap in baseline). Our proposed method achieves 85.99-88.99% cross-device accuracy and 90.85-92.58% same-device accuracy, significantly narrowing the performance gap. The AST demonstrates clear superiority over CNN baselines (VGGish, ResNet50) with 16-25% advantages. Ablation studies confirm that supervised contrastive learning and data augmentation each contribute 2-4% improvement, with synergistic benefits when combined. Robustness evaluation shows the proposed method maintains 82-87% accuracy even at 0dB SNR, while the baseline degrades dramatically. Few-shot learning experiments demonstrate remarkable data efficiency, achieving comparable performance with only 5 segments per session. These findings establish the feasibility of practical cross-device breath-sound biometrics with well-acceptable noise robustness and minimal enrollment requirements.

title: Integrated Active Suspension Control for Simultaneous Enhancement of Ride, Stability, and Acceleration
authors: Dong-Myoung Im, Hyuntae Cho, Seung-Han You, Wanki Cho
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Active suspension systems have evolved beyond their traditional role of improving ride comfort, yet integrated strategies that simultaneously enhance handling stability and driving performance remain underexplored. This paper proposes an integrated control algorithm for active suspension systems to enhance ride comfort and lateral stability, while specifically optimizing traction performance during cornering acceleration. The proposed control architecture consists of three modules: target motion generation, integrated control input calculation, and optimal force distribution. A key feature of this strategy is the formulation of an integrated control framework that determines the optimal distribution of active suspension forces to satisfy these diverse control objectives. The efficacy of the proposed controller is validated through a co-simulation environment using vehicle dynamics software. Simulation results demonstrate that the controller ensures effective tracking of target roll and pitch motions, while simultaneously improving yaw rate response. Crucially, in cornering acceleration scenarios based on real track data, the controller effectively increases the vertical load on inner driven wheels. This regulation minimizes excessive wheel slip and expands the vehicle’s operational envelope within the friction circle. Consequently, the proposed approach significantly enhances longitudinal acceleration and overall driving dynamics for both front- and rear-wheel-drive vehicles, confirming the potential of active suspension systems as a comprehensive chassis control solution.

authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification

title: Design and Control of a Multimodal Rotor-Assisted Pendulum-Driven Spherical Robot
authors: Dongshuai Huo, Hanxu Sun, Xiaojuan Lan, Minggang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: To address the inherent limitations of conventional pendulum-driven spherical robots (PDSRs), notably the chase-overshoot limit cycle oscillation (CLCO), slow dynamic response, and restricted terrain adaptability, this paper presents a rotor-assisted PDSR design, designated the Rotor-Assisted platform (RA). The proposed system augments a traditional two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) pendulum drive with a dual-rotor mechanism featuring passive opening/closing, tilting, and bidirectional rotation, enabling three locomotion modes (Mode 1–Mode 3). A rotor-pendulum cooperative control framework is developed, employing state-dependent torque allocation and an adaptive effectiveness matrix to achieve stable, coordinated actuation across all modes. Experimental results demonstrate that the rotor-assisted approach effectively mitigates this oscillation (with up to 82% reduction in pitch-rate root mean square (RMS)), significantly enhances attitude stability and maneuverability, and enables agile in-situ rotation as well as climbing on slopes up to 32<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> from a standstill. These capabilities validate RA as a versatile platform for dynamic, multi-terrain applications.

title: Influence of Epitaxial Layer Thickness on the Holding Voltage Scalability of Bi-directional SCR Devices
authors: Ting Yang, Jie Zeng, Qidong Xie, Kyong Jin Hwang, Tupei Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: This work investigates the impact of epitaxial layer (Epi) thickness on the holding voltage (Vh) scalability of bidirectional silicon-controlled rectifiers (BiSCRs) in 130 nm BCD technology. Experimental results show that increasing the base lengths (Lnb/Lpb) of parasitic BJTs leads to a linear increase in Vh under a thicker epitaxial process (1.5×), while having a negligible impact in a thinner epitaxial process (1×). Technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations attribute this disparity to Epi dependent current gain variations in the parasitic NPN and PNP BJTs: thinner Epi strengthens NPN gain, dominating conduction current and suppressing Vh control via lateral base-length adjustments, while thicker Epi weakens NPN gain, enabling effective Vh tuning. These findings demonstrate that Epi thickness critically determines the efficacy of geometric design strategies for BiSCR latch-up immunity, guiding optimized ESD protection in holding-voltage sensitive applications.

title: Robust Motor Decoding Using Distribution Alignment Without Recalibrating Intracortical Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: Jhih-Siang Huang, Chih-Hsuan Wu, Wei-Chen Chen, Yueh-Huan Lee, Shih-Hung Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Intracortical brain-computer interfaces (iBCIs) allow paralyzed individuals to regain motor functions by translating neural activity into control commands for assistive devices. However, neural variability caused by biological factors and electrode connectivity issues disrupts the mapping between neural signals and motor outputs, reducing decoding performance. While supervised recalibration of the neural decoder on subsequent days can mitigate this variability, the process often requires specialized personnel for operating recording systems and acquiring labeled data, which poses practical challenges. To address this issue, we propose Masked U-net-based Cycle-consistent Adversarial Networks (MUCAN), a framework designed to maintain the neuralto- kinematic mapping by aligning daily neural activity distributions with a reference day, thereby eliminating the need for recalibration. MUCAN employs a masking strategy to extract features that remain consistent across days, effectively mitigating daily variability in neural recording conditions. Additionally, it leverages the U-net architecture to extract hierarchical features and preserve fine-grained details of the neural activity, ensuring accurate distribution alignment. Experimental results demonstrate that MUCAN successfully maintains the neural-to-kinematic mapping and outperforms state-of-the-art iBCIs, achieving an average performance improvement of 15.69% across eight publicly available nonhuman primate datasets. MUCAN exhibits robustness against neural signal loss, even with 50 unexpectedly disabled recording channels. By projecting firing rates onto the first two principal components for visualizing latent trajectories, MUCAN effectively preserves the underlying structure of neural population activity over time. This study provides a robust approach to sustaining motor decoding performance without the necessity for continuous recalibration, enhancing the practicality of iBCIs for real-world applications.

title: Open Source Governance Practices: A Path to Improving Sustainability
authors: Dawn Foster
publish: Computer
abstract: This article provides advice and resources for improving open source project sustainability through good governance practices.
