title: Cross-Project Flakiness: A Case Study of the OpenStack Ecosystem
authors: Tao Xiao, Dong Wang, Shane McIntosh, Hideaki Hata, Yasutaka Kamei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering
abstract: Automated regression testing is a cornerstone of modern software development, often contributing directly to code review and Continuous Integration (CI). Yet some tests suffer from flakiness, where their outcomes vary non-deterministically. Flakiness erodes developer trust in test results, wastes computational resources, and undermines CI reliability. While prior research has examined test flakiness within individual projects, its broader ecosystem-wide impact remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present an empirical study of test flakiness in the OpenStack ecosystem, which focuses on (1) cross-project flakiness, where flaky tests impact multiple projects, and (2) inconsistent flakiness, where a test exhibits flakiness in some projects but remains stable in others. By analyzing 649 OpenStack projects, we identify 1,535 cross-project flaky tests and 1,105 inconsistently flaky tests. We find that cross-project flakiness affects 55% of OpenStack projects and significantly increases both review time and computational costs. Surprisingly, 70% of unit tests exhibit cross-project flakiness, challenging the assumption that unit tests are inherently insulated from issues that span modules like integration and system-level tests. Through qualitative analysis, we observe that race conditions in CI, inconsistent build configurations, and dependency mismatches are the primary causes of inconsistent flakiness. These findings underline the need for better coordination across complex ecosystems, standardized CI configurations, and improved test isolation strategies.

title: Sea Ice and Seawater Distribution Retrieval Using a Shipborne GNSS-Based Bistatic Radar System
authors: Wei Liu, Ziyu Hua, Yuan Hu, Zihui Lin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), with its all-weather availability and extensive spatial coverage, offers significant potential as an illuminator of opportunity for passive radar applications. Conventional GNSS reflectometry (GNSS-R) methods for sea-ice detection in polar regions primarily rely on delay– Doppler processing or dielectric-constant-based techniques. While delay– Doppler approaches are suitable for spaceborne observations of large-scale ice fields, they are less effective for detecting smaller ice floes. Dielectric-based methods can differentiate sea ice from seawater but cannot provide information on their spatial distribution. In this study, we propose a shipborne GNSS-based synthetic aperture radar (GNSS-SAR) imaging method to retrieve the spatial distribution of sea ice and seawater. A bistatic geometric configuration is first established, and the received signals are captured and tracked. The tracking-derived parameters are then used to construct noise-free reference and echo replicas, whose correlation yields two-dimensional compressed signals. Additionally, variations in the carrier-to-noise ratio (CNR) are exploited to distinguish between sea ice and seawater. High-resolution imaging is subsequently performed using the back-projection (BP) algorithm. Comparisons with the actual ice widths indicate errors of approximately 20%–30%. Validation against video footage from the research vessel Xuelong 2 confirms that the proposed method can clearly differentiate sea ice from seawater and reconstruct their approximate spatial distribution.

title: An Epistemic Uncertainty Aware Mamba Framework for Emotional Stress Assessment with Spontaneous Micro-Gestures
authors: Weijia Feng, Fengtao Sun, Xuejun Bai, Chenyang Wang, Xiaobao Wang, Jingyu Yang, Ruojia Zhang, Xiaobai Li, F. Richard Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Spontaneous micro-gestures (SMGs) are subtle, in voluntary body movements that reflect suppressed affective states and constitute an important source of behavioral evidence for emotional stress assessment. However, SMGs are extremely sparse and often kinematically indistinguishable from non affective background motions, making reliable affective inference fundamentally constrained by epistemic uncertainty rather than classification accuracy alone. In this paper, we propose EUA Mamba, an epistemic uncertainty-aware framework that reformulates SMG spotting and emotional stress assessment as an evidence-regulated affective inference process. Instead of treating SMGs as deterministic action patterns, the proposed approach explicitly models the sufficiency and reliability of behavioral evidence through a VFE-guided energy gating mechanism, enabling the system to actively veto ambiguous movements while preserving informative affective cues. To support continuous inference over hours-long behavioral streams, we adopt the Mamba state-space architecture with selective scan mechanisms. Experiments on a benchmark SMG dataset show that, under the IoU = 0.1 evaluation setting for affective biomarker localization, EUA-Mamba improves SMG spotting performance from an F1 score of 0.312 to 0.420, corresponding to a relative gain of 34.6%, while reducing false positives by over 60% in long-term recordings. The resulting SMG representations further support emotional stress assessment with an accuracy of 70.0%, exceeding human expert performance on skeletal modalities.

title: A Low Power Consumption Software PUF Based on Video Coding Hardware for Image Frame Encryption
authors: Tengfei Yuan, Pengjun Wang, Yuejun Zhang, Ziyu Zhou, Mingze Ren, Shuang Hu, Shutong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Compared with traditional video coding algorithms, video coding hardware circuits have higher coding efficiency, faster transmission rate, smaller storage space. They are widely used in the Internet of Thing (IoT). Aiming at the problems of encoding hardware protection and privacy data security of video encoding hardware under the resource constrained IoT. This paper proposes a low power consumption Software Physical Unclonable Function (SPUF) based on video coding hardware. The proposed SPUF generates video coding hardware feature response under low voltage, and implements image data coding and data compression and transmission under normal voltage. Firstly, we use the video encoding hardware in the H265 IP core. Secondly, the transmission delay characteristics of video coding hardware under different voltages are analyzed. Combined with the sampling output principle between register groups, the response is dependent on the delay of register path to generate unclonable random data under the low voltage. The SPUF was finally verified on TSMC 65nm. The results indicated that the SPUF response passed the NIST randomness test, with a randomness of 50.13%, an autocorrelation coefficient of 0.0181 at 95% confidence, and a power consumption of 78.38 fJ/bit. It has the characteristics of high security, low power consumption, and lightweight. To safeguard image-information transmission between IoT components and devices, this paper presents a lightweight encryption protocol founded on SPUF.

title: RobustFedX: Secure Aggregation of SHAP Explanations in Federated Intrusion Detection Against Byzantine Vehicles
authors: Walaa Alayed, Hassam Ahmed Tahir, Waqar Ul Hassan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Connected and autonomous vehicles increasingly employ federated learning (FL) for privacy-preserving collaborative intrusion detection. However, existing FL systems with explainable AI (XAI) assume honest participation in explanation aggregation, creating a critical vulnerability. We show that Byzantine vehicles can poison global interpretability maps by submitting falsified SHAP feature importance scores, suppressing security-critical indicators (e.g., packet length, CAN ID frequency) to evade detection. To address this threat, we propose RobustFedX, the first Byzantine-robust federated explainability framework securing both model parameters and explanation integrity via three complementary defenses: (1) geometric median-based robust aggregation with outlier detection for explanation vectors; (2) adversarial explanation training to ensure SHAP stability under perturbations; and (3) cryptographic hash commitments binding explanations to model parameters. Experiments on CICEVSE2024 and CICIoV2024 show that RobustFedX sustains 93.4% detection accuracy and 87.3% explanation fidelity under 30% Byzantine hybrid attacks, compared to 74.0% accuracy and 31.4% fidelity for prior XAI-FL-IDS methods. Ablation analysis confirms progressive robustness gains, achieving 95.9% attack resistance. With moderate overhead (50% training time, 24% communication), RobustFedX enables secure and transparent intrusion detection for safety-critical vehicular systems.

title: Adaptive Measurement Noise for Robust Kalman Filtering in Smart Beehive Telemetry
authors: H.A.A.U. Ranasinghe, Navod Neranjan, Jamil Abedalrahim Jamil Alsayaydeh, Rex Bacarra, Rostam Affendi Hamzah
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Honeybee colony monitoring generates multimodal, non-stationary telemetry streams that require reliable recursive state estimation with well-calibrated uncertainty for digital apiculture. Although Kalman filtering is widely used in environmental monitoring, adaptive measurement-noise modeling has not been systematically evaluated for smart-hive telemetry under leak-free chronological protocols. The approach performs online measurement-noise covariance adaptation using innovation statistics identified as the primary source of calibration improvement, while an innovation-based normalized innovation squared (NIS) gate serves as a secondary robustness safeguard against rare extreme observations. The framework is benchmarked against fixed-noise linear Kalman filtering, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), and ARIMA/SARIMAX baselines, with EKF and EnKF included primarily as stability reference baselines. Experiments are conducted using a strictly chronological train–validation–test replay protocol with one step-ahead forecasting on longitudinal data from instrumented honeybee colonies. Model performance is assessed using predictive accuracy, probabilistic calibration and innovation-based consistency diagnostics. Validation-based sensitivity and ablation analyses are included to evaluate robustness and to distinguish the contribution of adaptive measurement-noise estimation from the auxiliary robustness role of the normalized innovation squared (NIS) gating mechanism.

title: GCA-ResUNet: Medical Image Segmentation Using Grouped Coordinate Attention
authors: Jun Ding, Shang Gao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Accurate segmentation of heterogeneous anatomical structures is pivotal for computer-aided diagnosis and subsequent clinical decision-making. Although U-Net based convolutional neural networks have achieved remarkable progress, their intrinsic locality and largely homogeneous attention formulations often limit the modeling of long-range contextual dependencies, especially in multi-organ scenarios and low-contrast regions. Transformer-based architectures mitigate this issue by leveraging global self-attention, but they usually require higher computational resources and larger training data, which may impede deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments. In this paper, we propose GCA-ResUNet, an efficient medical image segmentation framework equipped with a lightweight and plug-and-play Grouped Coordinate Attention (GCA) module. The proposed GCA decouples channel-wise context modeling into multiple groups to explicitly account for semantic heterogeneity across channels, and integrates direction-aware coordinate encoding to capture structured spatial dependencies along horizontal and vertical axes. Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmarks, Synapse and ACDC, demonstrate that GCA-ResUNet achieves Dice scores of 86.11% and 92.64%, respectively, outperforming a range of representative CNN and Transformer-based methods, including Swin-UNet and TransUNet. In particular, GCA-ResUNet yields consistent improvements in delineating small anatomical structures with complex boundaries. These results indicate that the proposed approach provides a favorable trade-off between segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency, offering a practical and scalable solution for clinical deployment.
